u_2) = 1.2\times 10^{9}(0 - 1.667\times 10^{-5}) = -20\,000\;\text{N}$$ (Negative sign indicates the wall pushes back on the bar — equilibrium check: $R_1 + 20\,000 = 0$ ✓) --- ## 3.5 Important Properties of the 1D Bar Stiffness Matrix | Property | Statement | Implication | |---|---|---| | **Symmetry** | $[k]^T = [k]$ | Betti's reciprocity; saves CPU time | | **Singularity** | $\det[k] = 0$ | Rigid-body mode exists; BCs essential | | **Positive Semi-Definite** | All eigenvalues $\geq 0$ | Energy non-negativity | | **Rank** | 1 (out of 2) | One independent equilibrium equation per element | | **Bandwidth** | 1 | Tridiagonal global $[K]$ for chained bars | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The One-Dimensional Bar Element — Physical Representation ``` u1 ┌───────┐ u2 ●────│ BAR │────● │ │ E,A,L │ │ F1 │ │ F2 ↓ └───────┘ ↓ Node i Node j (x = 0) (x = L) ``` **Description:** A prismatic bar of length $L$, constant cross-section $A$, and Young's modulus $E$. Two end nodes, each with one axial DOF ($u_1, u_2$) and one axial nodal force ($F_1, F_2$). --- ## 4.2 FE Direct Approach — Methodology Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STEP 1: Discretize Domain into Elements"] --> B["STEP 2: Assume Displacement Field"] B --> C["STEP 3: Derive B-Matrix from Kinematics"] C --> D["STEP 4: Apply Constitutive Law sigma = E * epsilon"] D --> E["STEP 5: Apply Equilibrium / Virtual Work"] E --> F["STEP 6: Assemble Element Stiffness k"] F --> G["STEP 7: Assemble Global K and F"] G --> H["STEP 8: Apply Boundary Conditions"] H --> I["STEP 9: Solve K d = F for nodal d"] I --> J["STEP 10: Post-Process strain, stress, reaction"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Two-Element Assembly — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["Node 1
u1 = 0 (Fixed)"] --- elem1["Element 1
k1 = A1E/L1
[ 1 -1 ]
[ -1 1 ]"] elem1 --- node2["Node 2
u2 = ?
Shared DOF"] node2 --- elem2["Element 2
k2 = A2E/L2
[ 1 -1 ]
[ -1 1 ]"] elem2 --- node3["Node 3
u3 = ?
Load P"] node3 --> loadP["Applied Force P"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — From PDE to Discrete System ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CONTINUUM["CONTINUUM DOMAIN"] PDE["Strong Form PDE
d/dx(EA du/dx) + Ab = 0"] end subgraph FE_FORMULATION["FE DIRECT FORMULATION"] SHAPE["Shape Functions
N1 = 1 - x/L
N2 = x/L"] B["B-Matrix
B = 1/L [ -1 1 ]"] KFORM["Stiffness Integral
k = integral of B^T E A B dx"] FFORM["Load Vector
Fb = integral of N^T A b dx"] end subgraph DISCRETE["DISCRETE SYSTEM"] GLOB["Global Assembly [K], {F}"] BC["Apply BCs u_1 = 0"] SOLVE["Solve [K]{d} = {F}"] POST["Post-process sigma, eps, R"] end PDE --> SHAPE SHAPE --> B B --> KFORM SHAPE --> FFORM KFORM --> GLOB FFORM --> GLOB GLOB --> BC BC --> SOLVE SOLVE --> POST ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Define a 1D bar element and state the number of degrees of freedom per node. **Model Answer:** A one-dimensional bar element is a straight, prismatic structural member that carries only axial loading. It has two end nodes (node $i$ at $x=0$ and node $j$ at $x=L$), and each node possesses **one translational degree of freedom** — the axial displacement $u$. Therefore, the total DOFs per element = 2. The element is characterized by length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** Write down the shape functions for a linear 1D bar element and state their key property. **Model Answer:** For a bar element of length $L$ with local coordinate $x$ measured from node 1: $$N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \qquad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}$$ **Key properties:** - **Kronecker-delta property:** $N_1(0)=1,\; N_1(L)=0,\; N_2(0)=0,\; N_2(L)=1$. - **Partition-of-unity:** $N_1(x) + N_2(x) = 1$ for all $x \in [0, L]$. - **Linear completeness:** They can represent any rigid-body translation and constant strain exactly. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > > [!WARNING] > > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor $A$** in $k = AE/L$ — losing 2 marks. > > 2. **Not stating the B-matrix explicitly** — losing 1 mark. > > 3. **Incorrect assembly at shared node** (off-by-one error) — losing 3 marks. > > 4. **Applying BCs AFTER solving instead of partitioning $[K]$** — losing 2 marks. > > 5. **Forgetting to verify equilibrium** (reaction check) — losing 1 mark. --- ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question A]** **(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix $[k]$ and consistent load vector $\{F_b\}$ for a one-dimensional bar element using the direct stiffness approach. Assume uniform body force $b$ (per unit volume) and constant $EA$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Assume linear displacement field:** $$u(x) = N_1(x) u_1 + N_2(x) u_2 = \left(1-\frac{x}{L}\right)u_1 + \frac{x}{L} u_2$$ **Step 2 — Strain (kinematics):** $$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{du}{dx} = -\frac{u_1}{L} + \frac{u_2}{L} = \mathbf{B}\{d\} = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix}-1 & 1\end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Step 3 — Stress (constitutive):** $$\sigma(x) = E\varepsilon(x)$$ **Step 4 — Apply principle of virtual work:** $$\int_0^L (\delta\varepsilon)\, \sigma\, A\,dx = \int_0^L (\delta u)\, b\, A\,dx$$ $$\delta\{d\}^T \!\left[\int_0^L \mathbf{B}^T E A \mathbf{B}\, dx\right]\{d\} = \delta\{d\}^T \!\left[\int_0^L \mathbf{N}^T A b\, dx\right]$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate integrals:** $$ \begin{aligned} [k] &= \frac{EA}{L^2}\!\int_0^L\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\!dx = \frac{EA}{L}\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[8pt] \{F_b\} &= Ab\!\int_0^L\!\begin{Bmatrix} 1 - x/L \\ x/L \end{Bmatrix}\!dx = \frac{AbL}{2}\!\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Hence the element equilibrium equation is: $$\frac{EA}{L}\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{AbL}{2}\!\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **(7 marks — Full marks for clear B-matrix, integration steps, and final boxed result.)** --- **(b)** A stepped bar is composed of two elements fixed at node 1. Element 1 has $A_1 = 1000\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_1 = 200\,\text{mm}$; Element 2 has $A_2 = 500\,\text{mm}^2$, $L_2 = 100\,\text{mm}$. A point load $P = 10\,\text{kN}$ acts at node 3. Take $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$. Find nodal displacements and element stresses. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Element stiffnesses:** $$k_1 = \frac{1000\times 10^{-6}\times 200\times 10^{9}}{0.2} = 1.0\times 10^{9}\;\text{N/m}$$ $$k_2 = \frac{500\times 10^{-6}\times 200\times 10^{9}}{0.1} = 1.0\times 10^{9}\;\text{N/m}$$ **Step 2 — Global stiffness matrix (3 × 3):** $$[K] = 10^{9}\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{N/m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply BC $u_1 = 0$, partition:** $$10^{9}\!\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\!\begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 10\,000 \end{Bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Solve:** Determinant $= (2)(1) - (-1)(-1) = 1$ $$ \begin{aligned} u_3 &= \frac{(2)(10\,000) - (-1)(0)}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 2.0\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} \\[6pt] u_2 &= \frac{(1)(0) - (-1)(10\,000)}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 1.0\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Element stresses:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_1 &= \frac{E}{L_1}(u_2 - u_1) = \frac{200\times 10^{9}}{0.2}(1.0\times 10^{-5}) = 10\;\text{MPa} \\[6pt] \sigma_2 &= \frac{E}{L_2}(u_3 - u_2) = \frac{200\times 10^{9}}{0.1}(2.0\times 10^{-5} - 1.0\times 10^{-5}) = 20\;\text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Equilibrium check:** Reaction $R_1 = -k_1 u_2 = -1.0\times 10^{9}(1.0\times 10^{-5}) = -10\,\text{kN}$ ✓ **(7 marks — Show assembly, partitioning, solution, and equilibrium verification.)** --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question B]** **(a)** Explain the various steps involved in the direct FE formulation approach for a 1D bar element. State clearly the role of the B-matrix, D-matrix (constitutive), and the assembly process. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **Direct Approach** follows these systematic steps: 1. **Discretization:** Divide the bar into $n$ elements connected at $n+1$ nodes. Each element: length $L^{(e)}$, area $A^{(e)}$, modulus $E^{(e)}$. 2. **Displacement Model:** Assume a polynomial that satisfies the minimum completeness requirement (linear for bar): $$u^{(e)}(x) = N_1(x) u_1^{(e)} + N_2(x) u_2^{(e)}$$ 3. **Strain–Displacement (B-matrix):** Differentiate $u$ w.r.t. $x$: $$\mathbf{B} = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix}-1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$ *Role:* Maps nodal DOFs to element strain — the kinematic operator. 4. **Constitutive (D-matrix):** For 1D linear elastic bar: $$\mathbf{D} = [E]$$ *Role:* Maps strain to stress via Hooke's law. 5. **Stiffness Integral:** $$[k^{(e)}] = \int_{V^{(e)}} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D}\,\mathbf{B}\,dV = \frac{AE}{L}\!\begin{bmatrix}1 & -1\\-1 & 1\end{bmatrix}$$ 6. **Load Vector:** $$\{F^{(e)}\} = \int_{V^{(e)}} \mathbf{N}^T b\,dV + \int_{S_t^{(e)}} \mathbf{N}^T T\,dS$$ 7. **Assembly:** Global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ built by summing element contributions at shared nodes: $$K_{ij} = \sum_{e=1}^{n} k_{ij}^{(e)}$$ *Role of assembly:* Enforces displacement compatibility at shared nodes and global equilibrium. 8. **Boundary Conditions & Solution:** Apply essential BCs, partition $[K]$, solve $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$. 9. **Post-Processing:** Compute element strain, stress, and reactions. **(7 marks — 1 mark each for steps 1–4, 1.5 marks each for steps 5–7, 1 mark for post-processing.)** --- **(b)** A uniform bar of length $L = 1$ m, area $A = 200\,\text{mm}^2$, and $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$ is fixed at both ends and subjected to a point load $P = 5\,\text{kN}$ at its mid-node. Model with two equal elements and find the nodal displacements, reactions, and element stress. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Setup:** Nodes 1–2 (Element 1) and 2–3 (Element 2). Length each $L/2 = 0.5$ m. **Stiffness per element:** $$k = \frac{AE}{L/2} = \frac{200\times 10^{-6}\times 200\times 10^{9}}{0.5} = 8.0\times 10^{7}\;\text{N/m}$$ **Global stiffness (symmetric, tridiagonal):** $$[K] = 8.0\times 10^{7}\!\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{N/m}$$ **BCs:** $u_1 = 0$, $u_3 = 0$; load $F_2 = 5000$ N at node 2. **Reduced system:** $$8.0\times 10^{7}\cdot(2)\,u_2 = 5000 \;\Rightarrow\; u_2 = \frac{5000}{1.6\times 10^{8}} = 3.125\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}$$ **Reactions:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_1 &= -k(u_2 - u_1) = -8.0\times 10^{7}(3.125\times 10^{-5}) = -2500\;\text{N} \\[6pt] R_3 &= -k(u_2 - u_3) = -8.0\times 10^{7}(3.125\times 10^{-5}) = -2500\;\text{N} \end{aligned} $$ (Negative indicates walls pull inward. Sum: $-2500 - 2500 + 5000 = 0$ ✓) **Element stresses (both symmetric, equal):** $$\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \frac{E}{L/2}(u_2 - u_1) = \frac{200\times 10^{9}}{0.5}(3.125\times 10^{-5}) = 12.5\;\text{MPa}$$ **(7 marks — Full derivation with assembly, reduction, and verification.)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Things to Remember (Exam-Day Survival List):** - [x] **Element stiffness matrix formula** — Memorize: $[k] = \dfrac{AE}{L}\!\begin{bmatrix}1&-1\\-1&1\end{bmatrix}$ — this alone fetches 3–5 marks. - [x] **Shape functions** — $N_1 = 1 - x/L$, $N_2 = x/L$ — always write them first. - [x] **B-matrix** — $\mathbf{B} = \dfrac{1}{L}[-1\;\;\;1]$ — constant for linear bar. - [x] **D-matrix** — $\mathbf{D} = [E]$ for 1D. - [x] **Body-force vector** — $\{F_b\} = \dfrac{AbL}{2}\{1\;\;1\}^T$ for uniform $b$. - [x] **Assembly rule** — Add element $k_{ij}$ contributions to global $K_{ij}$ for every shared DOF. - [x] **Always check singularity** — Det$[k] = 0$ means rigid-body mode; apply BCs before inverting. - [x] **Symmetry of $[K]$** — Use only upper/lower triangle to save computation. - [x] **Units check** — $k$ has units N/m; $F$ in N; $u$ in m; $E$ in Pa; $A$ in $\text{m}^2$; $L$ in m. - [x] **Reaction formula** — $R_i = k_i(u_i^{\text{fixed}} - u_i^{\text{free}})$ at constrained DOFs. - [x] **Stress in element** — $\sigma = \dfrac{E}{L}(u_j - u_i)$ — independent of area. - [x] **Strain compatibility** — At shared node, displacement is unique (continuity enforced by assembly). - [x] **Thermal load** (if asked) — $\{F_{th}\} = EA\alpha\Delta T\{-1\;\;\;1\}^T$. - [x] **Energy equivalence** — Strain energy $U = \tfrac{1}{2}\{d\}^T[k]\{d\}$; for multiple elements, sum element $U$'s.

topics.title: Assembly of Elements
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Assembly of Elements** is the systematic procedure in the **Direct Stiffness Method (DSM)** wherein the individual element stiffness matrices $[k]^e$ and element load vectors $\{f\}^e$ — derived in local/global coordinates — are systematically combined using a **connectivity (topology) mapping** to construct the **Global Stiffness Matrix** $[K]$ and the **Global Load Vector** $\{F\}$ of the entire discretized domain.

Mathematically, the assembled governing equilibrium equation is expressed as:

$$
[K] \, \{U\} = \{F\}
$$

where $[K]$ is the assembled global stiffness matrix of order $n \times n$ (with $n$ equal to the total number of active degrees of freedom in the model), $\{U\}$ is the global nodal displacement vector, and $\{F\}$ is the global nodal force vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Assembly is the *bridge step* between element-level formulation (Module 1) and the solution of the global linear system. The mapping between *local DOFs* of an element and *global DOFs* of the structure is the single most important concept for the university exam.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Brick Cathedral"

Imagine you are building a giant cathedral using thousands of individual **Lego bricks**.

- Each Lego brick is a **finite element**. Each brick is small, simple, and easy to analyze on its own (you already know its stiffness — how it bends, twists, or stretches).
- Every brick has a small set of **studs/connection points** where it can be joined to neighboring bricks. These correspond to the **nodes** of the element, and the studs represent the element's **degrees of freedom (DOFs)**.
- The instruction manual of the Lego set is the **connectivity matrix** (also called the *LM* or *incidence matrix*). It tells you exactly *which local node of which brick connects to which global node of the cathedral*.
- When you physically click bricks together, the studs at the shared connection points must coincide — this is the **compatibility condition** that drives the assembly.
- The completed cathedral is the **assembled global structure**, whose overall stiffness is far greater and far more complex than any individual brick.

**Assembly** is the act of clicking every brick (element) into its correct global position and *summing up* the stiffness contributions at each shared stud (node) to obtain the total structural stiffness.

## 1.3 Why Is Assembly Necessary?

In the Finite Element Method, we never derive one gigantic stiffness matrix for the whole structure in one shot. Instead, we exploit the principle of **superposition** and the **piecewise approximation** nature of the FEM:

1. We solve a *small*, well-posed problem on each element (using shape functions and weak form).
2. We exploit the fact that most elements **do not interact directly** — they only share degrees of freedom at common nodes.
3. Therefore, we can sum each element's contribution to the *global* equations *node by node*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The element stiffness matrix $[k]^e$ has size $n_e \times n_e$ (e.g., $8 \times 8$ for a 4-node quad, $12 \times 12$ for a 3D beam). The global matrix $[K]$ has size $n \times n$ where $n$ is the total DOF of the structure. The assembly procedure is what links the two scales.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisites Before Assembly

Before assembly can be performed, the following quantities must be **fully computed and ready**:

| # | Pre-requisite Quantity | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|------------------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Element stiffness matrix (global coordinates) | $[k]^e$ | Local stiffness contribution |
| 2 | Element load/force vector | $\{f\}^e$ | Local force contribution |
| 3 | Element degrees of freedom array (LM vector) | $\Delta^e$ or $\{LM\}^e$ | Maps local DOF → global DOF |
| 4 | Total number of global DOFs | $N$ | Sets the size of $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ |
| 5 | Boundary conditions (essential/Dirichlet) | prescribed $U$ | Used *after* assembly |

## 2.2 The Location Matrix (LM / Connectivity / Topology Matrix)

The single most important data structure that *drives* assembly is the **Location Matrix of Element $e$**, denoted $\Delta^e$ or $\{LM\}^e$. For a structure with $N$ global DOFs, it is a column vector of length $n_e$ (the number of DOFs in element $e$).

> **General form:**
$$
\Delta^e = \begin{Bmatrix} d_1^e \\ d_2^e \\ \vdots \\ d_{n_e}^e \end{Bmatrix}
$$
where $d_i^e$ is the *global DOF number* corresponding to the $i$-th local DOF of element $e$.

**Worked Mini-Example (1D Spring with 2 DOFs per node):**

Consider a 1D bar divided into 2 elements with 3 nodes. The DOFs are $\{u_1, u_2, u_3\}$ so $N = 3$. Each element has 2 DOFs.

- Element 1 connects nodes 1 and 2 → $\Delta^1 = \{1, 2\}^T$
- Element 2 connects nodes 2 and 3 → $\Delta^2 = \{2, 3\}^T$

> [!NOTE]
> $\Delta^e$ is *not* assembled itself — it is the *recipe* that tells the algorithm *where* to place $[k]^e$ inside the global $[K]$.

## 2.3 The Direct Stiffness Assembly Algorithm

The assembly is performed by **additive superposition**. The formal rule is:

$$
K_{\Delta^e_i, \, \Delta^e_j} \;\leftarrow\; K_{\Delta^e_i, \, \Delta^e_j} \;+\; k^e_{i,j}
$$

$$
F_{\Delta^e_i} \;\leftarrow\; F_{\Delta^e_i} \;+\; f^e_{i}
$$

These summations are performed for **every element $e$** in the mesh, and for **every local index pair $(i,j)$** within element $e$.

### 2.3.1 Algorithm (Pseudocode in Words — No Programming)

**Step 1 — Initialize.** Create a zero matrix $[K]$ of size $N \times N$ and a zero vector $\{F\}$ of size $N$. This is the most common pitfall area.

**Step 2 — Loop over elements.** For each element $e = 1, 2, \ldots, E$:

- Read the location matrix $\Delta^e$.
- Compute the element stiffness matrix $[k]^e$ in global coordinates.
- Compute the element load vector $\{f\}^e$.

**Step 3 — Local-to-global scatter (additive).** For $i = 1$ to $n_e$ and for $j = 1$ to $n_e$:

- Compute global row index: $I = \Delta^e_i$
- Compute global column index: $J = \Delta^e_j$
- Add: $K_{I,J} = K_{I,J} + k^e_{i,j}$
- Add: $F_I = F_I + f^e_i$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions.** After assembly, apply essential (Dirichlet) BCs.

**Step 5 — Solve.** Solve $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ for $\{U\}$.

**Step 6 — Post-Process.** Recover element stresses/strains from the global displacements.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity / Operation | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|----------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Global equilibrium equation | $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ | Final assembled system |
| 2 | Size of global matrix | $N = \text{total DOFs in model}$ | E.g. 2D truss: $2 \times \text{nodes}$ |
| 3 | Element size | $n_e$ | 4 (bar), 6 (tri), 8 (quad), 12 (tet), 24 (hex) |
| 4 | Additive assembly rule (stiffness) | $K_{I,J} = K_{I,J} + k^e_{i,j}$ | $I = \Delta^e_i$, $J = \Delta^e_j$ |
| 5 | Additive assembly rule (force) | $F_I = F_I + f^e_i$ | $I = \Delta^e_i$ |
| 6 | Boundedness (banded property) | $\text{bw}(K) = \max_{e}(\Delta^e_{\max} - \Delta^e_{\min} + n^e_{\text{row}})$ | Function of numbering |
| 7 | Symmetry | $K_{IJ} = K_{JI}$ | Always symmetric for conservative systems |
| 8 | Singular before BCs | $\det([K]_{\text{unsupported}}) = 0$ | Rigid body modes exist |
| 9 | Sparsity | fraction of nonzero entries $\ll 1$ | For large $N$ |
| 10 | Penalty / Elimination BC | $K_{ii} \rightarrow \alpha,\; F_i \rightarrow \alpha \, u_i$ | Penalty method |
| 11 | Elimination BC (matrix) | Row $i$, col $i$ zeroed; set $K_{ii} = 1, F_i = u_i$ | Direct method |
| 12 | Half-bandwidth | $H = D + 1$ | $D$ = max nodal DOF difference |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Assembly

| Engineering Field | Utility of Robust Assembly |
|-------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Structural (Civil/Mech)** | Models bridges, buildings, pressure vessels with millions of DOFs |
| **Aerospace** | Wing-box and fuselage analyses with composite layups |
| **Biomedical** | Bone-implant stress analysis with patient-specific geometry |
| **Automotive (Crash)** | Time-history explicit assembly in LS-DYNA, ABAQUS/Explicit |
| **Geomechanics** | Tunnels, dams, slopes with thousands of soil elements |
| **Thermal** | Identical math; assemble conductance matrix $[C]\{T\} = \{Q\}$ |

## 2.6 Properties of the Assembled Global Stiffness Matrix

1. **Symmetry** — $K_{IJ} = K_{JI}$. Always true for linear, conservative systems (follows from Betti's reciprocal theorem).
2. **Singularity** — $\det([K]) = 0$ *before* boundary conditions. The structure can undergo rigid body motion.
3. **Sparsity** — For a mesh of $N$ DOFs, $[K]$ is mostly zeros (e.g. 0.1% nonzero for a 2D plane stress model).
4. **Banded** — If nodes are numbered *optimally*, $[K]$ has a narrow band of nonzeros around the main diagonal.
5. **Positive Semi-Definite** — All eigenvalues $\lambda \geq 0$ (after applying sufficient BCs, becomes **positive definite**).

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error: asserting $[K]$ is positive definite *before* applying boundary conditions. The matrix is *singular* (positive *semi*-definite) until sufficient essential BCs are imposed to remove rigid body modes.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Additive Assembly Rule from Superposition

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Principle

Let the structure be discretized into $E$ elements. By the **principle of virtual work** applied to the global domain, the total internal virtual work equals the total external virtual work:

$$
\sum_{e=1}^{E} \delta \Pi_{\text{int}}^e = \sum_{e=1}^{E} \delta \Pi_{\text{ext}}^e
$$

### 3.1.2 Element-Level Equation (Already Derived in Module 1)

For a single element $e$, after applying the weak form and Gauss quadrature, the element-level equilibrium is:

$$
[k]^e \, \{d\}^e = \{f\}^e + \{f\}^{e,\text{body}} + \{f\}^{e,\text{traction}}
$$

where $\{d\}^e$ is the local nodal displacement vector of length $n_e$.

### 3.1.3 Mapping Local DOFs to Global DOFs

We can write the relationship between local and global displacement vectors through the **Boolean/Boolean-like location matrix** $L^e$ (a $N \times n_e$ matrix whose only nonzero entry in column $i$ is 1 at row $\Delta^e_i$):

$$
\{d\}^e = [L^e]^T \{U\}
$$

### 3.1.4 Substituting into the Element Equation

Multiplying both sides of the element equilibrium by $L^e$ (or equivalently, by the transpose of the local-to-global operator):

$$
[L^e] \, [k]^e \, [L^e]^T \{U\} = [L^e] \{f\}^e
$$

Define the **global element contribution matrices**:

$$
[K]^e = [L^e] \, [k]^e \, [L^e]^T \quad \text{(size } N \times N \text{)}
$$

$$
\{F\}^e = [L^e] \{f\}^e \quad \text{(size } N \text{)}
$$

### 3.1.5 Summing Over All Elements

Summing the local contributions of all elements:

$$
\left( \sum_{e=1}^{E} [K]^e \right) \{U\} = \sum_{e=1}^{E} \{F\}^e
$$

Hence, the assembled global matrix and vector are:

$$
[K] = \sum_{e=1}^{E} [K]^e = \sum_{e=1}^{E} [L^e] [k]^e [L^e]^T
$$

$$
\{F\} = \sum_{e=1}^{E} \{F\}^e = \sum_{e=1}^{E} [L^e] \{f\}^e
$$

### 3.1.6 Component-Wise (Index Notation) Form

The component-wise assembly rule, which is the *operational* form used in hand calculations, follows directly:

$$
K_{IJ} = \sum_{e=1}^{E} k^e_{i,j} \, \delta_{I, \Delta^e_i} \, \delta_{J, \Delta^e_j}
$$

In plain words: **add $k^e_{i,j}$ to the $(I, J)$ entry of the global matrix, where $I = \Delta^e_i$ and $J = \Delta^e_j$**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 1D Bar with 3 Springs in Series

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A 1D bar is discretized into 2 elements connected at 3 nodes. The element stiffness values (in N/m) are $k^{(1)} = 100$ and $k^{(2)} = 200$. Node 1 is fixed ($u_1 = 0$); a force $F = 300$ N is applied at node 3. Find the global $[K]$ and $\{F\}$ and solve.

### 3.2.2 Local Element Matrices

Each spring has local stiffness:

$$
[k]^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & -100 \\ -100 & 100 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad [k]^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 200 & -200 \\ -200 & 200 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.3 Location Matrices

- Element 1 (nodes 1, 2): $\Delta^{(1)} = \{1, 2\}^T$
- Element 2 (nodes 2, 3): $\Delta^{(2)} = \{2, 3\}^T$

### 3.2.4 Assembly (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

Initialize a $3 \times 3$ zero matrix $[K] = \mathbf{0}_3$ and a $3 \times 1$ zero vector $\{F\} = \mathbf{0}_3$.

**For Element 1:**

- $i = 1, j = 1$: $I = \Delta^{(1)}_1 = 1$, $J = \Delta^{(1)}_1 = 1$. Add $k^{(1)}_{1,1} = 100$:

$$
K_{1,1} = 0 + 100 = 100
$$

- $i = 1, j = 2$: $I = 1$, $J = 2$. Add $k^{(1)}_{1,2} = -100$:

$$
K_{1,2} = 0 + (-100) = -100
$$

- $i = 2, j = 1$: $I = 2$, $J = 1$. Add $k^{(1)}_{2,1} = -100$:

$$
K_{2,1} = 0 + (-100) = -100
$$

- $i = 2, j = 2$: $I = 2$, $J = 2$. Add $k^{(1)}_{2,2} = 100$:

$$
K_{2,2} = 0 + 100 = 100
$$

**For Element 2:**

- $i = 1, j = 1$: $I = \Delta^{(2)}_1 = 2$, $J = 2$. Add $k^{(2)}_{1,1} = 200$:

$$
K_{2,2} = 100 + 200 = 300
$$

- $i = 1, j = 2$: $I = 2$, $J = 3$. Add $k^{(2)}_{1,2} = -200$:

$$
K_{2,3} = 0 + (-200) = -200
$$

- $i = 2, j = 1$: $I = 3$, $J = 2$. Add $k^{(2)}_{2,1} = -200$:

$$
K_{3,2} = 0 + (-200) = -200
$$

- $i = 2, j = 2$: $I = 3$, $J = 3$. Add $k^{(2)}_{2,2} = 200$:

$$
K_{3,3} = 0 + 200 = 200
$$

### 3.2.5 Assembled Global Matrix

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & -100 & 0 \\ -100 & 300 & -200 \\ 0 & -200 & 200 \end{bmatrix} \;\text{N/m}
$$

### 3.2.6 Global Load Vector

$$
\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 300 \end{Bmatrix} \;\text{N}
$$

### 3.2.7 Apply Boundary Condition ($u_1 = 0$)

Eliminate row 1 and column 1. The reduced system is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 300 & -200 \\ -200 & 200 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 300 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

### 3.2.8 Solve (Cramer's Rule)

Determinant:

$$
\det = (300)(200) - (-200)(-200) = 60000 - 40000 = 20000
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{(0)(200) - (-200)(300)}{20000} = \frac{60000}{20000} = 3 \;\text{m}
$$

$$
u_3 = \frac{(300)(300) - (0)(-200)}{20000} = \frac{90000}{20000} = 4.5 \;\text{m}
$$

### 3.2.9 Verification — Physical Intuition Check

In a series spring, the equivalent stiffness is:

$$
k_{\text{eq}} = \left( \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} \right)^{-1} = \left( \frac{1}{100} + \frac{1}{200} \right)^{-1} = \frac{200}{3} \approx 66.67 \;\text{N/m}
$$

Displacement at node 3 (free end):

$$
u_3 = \frac{F}{k_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{300}{200/3} = \frac{900}{200} = 4.5 \;\text{m} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The match $u_3 = 4.5$ m from the assembled matrix and the direct series formula **validates the assembly procedure**. This is the gold-standard KTU verification check.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix for Hand Calculations — Stepwise Template

For any 2D truss problem in the KTU exam, the analytical matrix used is:

| Step | Operation | Output |
|------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Compute $[k]^e$ in local coordinates | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 2 | Compute transformation matrix $[T]^e$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 3 | Compute $[k]^e_{\text{global}} = [T]^e{}^T [k]^e [T]^e$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 4 | Write $\Delta^e$ for element | Column vector of 4 entries |
| 5 | Scatter entries into $4 \times 4$ blocks of $[K]$ | $2n \times 2n$ matrix |
| 6 | Assemble $\{F\}$ | $2n \times 1$ vector |
| 7 | Apply BCs | Reduced system |
| 8 | Solve $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ | Nodal displacements |
| 9 | Back-substitute: $\{d\}^e = [T]^e \{U^e\}$ | Element local displacements |
| 10 | Compute element forces | Internal forces |

## 3.4 Derivation of Banded Structure (Why Banded?)

The bandwidth $B$ of the assembled matrix is determined by the **maximum difference** between global DOF numbers of any single element:

$$
B = \max_{e} \left( \Delta^e_{\max} - \Delta^e_{\min} + 1 \right)
$$

**Proof Sketch:** All nonzeros of $[K]^e = [L^e][k]^e [L^e]^T$ lie in the submatrix defined by rows/columns $\Delta^e_{\min}$ to $\Delta^e_{\max}$. The union of these submatrices forms a band of half-width $B$ around the diagonal. Outside this band, all entries are exactly zero because no element has a coupling between DOFs whose numbers differ by more than $B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** "Show that re-numbering nodes can change the bandwidth but NOT the number of nonzeros." This is a frequently asked 7-mark question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Assembly Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Mesh and Material Data] --> A1[Initialize Global K and F as Zero]
    A1 --> A2[Loop over element e from 1 to E]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Element Stiffness k in Local Coords]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Transformation Matrix T for element e]
    A4 --> A5[Transform k_global = T transpose k T]
    A5 --> A6[Compute Element Load Vector f]
    A6 --> A7[Read Location Matrix LM for element e]
    A7 --> A8[Scatter Loop: for i and j in element DOFs]
    A8 --> A9[K at LM_i, LM_j = K at LM_i, LM_j + k_global_ij]
    A9 --> A10[F at LM_i = F at LM_i + f_i]
    A10 --> A11{More elements?}
    A11 -- Yes --> A2
    A11 -- No --> A12[Assembly of K and F Complete]
    A12 --> A13[Apply Essential Boundary Conditions]
    A13 --> A14[Reduce System Size]
    A14 --> A15[Solve K U = F for U]
    A15 --> A16[Post-Process: Stresses and Reactions]
    A16 --> A17[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Assembly Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        N1[Node Coordinates]
        N2[Element Connectivity LM Matrix]
        N3[Material Properties E nu]
        N4[Element Types]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Assembly Engine]
        B1[Element Stiffness Module]
        B2[Transformation Module]
        B3[Scatter and Add Module]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Global Stiffness K]
        C2[Global Force F]
        C3[Boundary Condition Handler]
        C4[Linear Solver]
    end
    N1 --> B1
    N2 --> B1
    N3 --> B1
    N4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    N2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> SOL[Displacement U]
    SOL --> POST[Post Processing Stresses]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Storage Schemes for Sparse Global Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Sparse Global Stiffness K] --> S1{Storage Scheme}
    S1 -- Optimized for Solver --> S2[Full Sparse CSR CSC]
    S1 -- Optimized for Skyline --> S3[Column Wise Skyline]
    S1 -- Optimized for Banded --> S4[Banded Storage LAPACK]
    S2 --> S2A[Direct Sparse Solvers PARDISO MUMPS]
    S3 --> S3A[Skyline Cholesky LDLT]
    S4 --> S4A[LAPACK DGBTRF DGBTRS]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Boundary Condition Application

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Assembled
    Assembled --> SingularCheck: K is singular before BCs
    SingularCheck --> ApplyBC: Det K is zero
    ApplyBC --> PenaltyMethod: Add large alpha to K_ii
    ApplyBC --> EliminationMethod: Zero out row col and set K_ii to 1
    PenaltyMethod --> PositiveDefinite
    EliminationMethod --> PositiveDefinite
    PositiveDefinite --> SolveKUF
    SolveKUF --> DisplacementsU
    DisplacementsU --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Scattering of Element Matrix into Global Matrix

A schematic representation of how the $2 \times 2$ element matrix of a 2-node bar is placed inside a $3 \times 3$ global matrix:

```
        Global DOF:   1     2     3
                   +-----+-----+-----+
        Row 1  :    | 100 | -100|  0  |
                   +-----+-----+-----+
        Row 2  :    |-100 | 300 |-200 |
                   +-----+-----+-----+
        Row 3  :    |  0  |-200 | 200 |
                   +-----+-----+-----+

        Element 1 (rows 1,2) -> adds 100,-100,-100,100 to top-left 2x2 block.
        Element 2 (rows 2,3) -> adds 200,-200,-200,200 to bottom-right 2x2 block.
        Overlap at K_22 -> 100 + 200 = 300.
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "assembly" as used in the Finite Element Method. What is the role of the location matrix in this process? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Assembly is the process by which element stiffness matrices $[k]^e$ and element load vectors $\{f\}^e$ are combined, using their location (connectivity) information, to form the global stiffness matrix $[K]$ and global load vector $\{F\}$ of the structure.
>
> The **location matrix (LM)** or **connectivity array** $\Delta^e$ maps the *local* degrees of freedom of each element to their corresponding *global* degrees of freedom in the model. Without the LM, the assembler has no way of knowing *where* to add each element's contribution inside the global matrix. The LM enables the **additive scatter rule** $K_{I,J} \leftarrow K_{I,J} + k^e_{i,j}$, where $I = \Delta^e_i$ and $J = \Delta^e_j$.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four properties of the assembled global stiffness matrix $[K]$. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Symmetry:** $K_{IJ} = K_{JI}$ for linear, conservative systems (Betti's reciprocal theorem).
2. **Singularity before BCs:** $\det([K]) = 0$ in the absence of sufficient essential boundary conditions, because rigid body modes are admissible.
3. **Positive semi-definiteness:** All eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$. Becomes positive definite only after applying enough BCs.
4. **Sparsity:** The number of nonzeros grows linearly with $N$ (in well-conditioned meshes) rather than as $N^2$.
5. **Banded structure:** With optimal node numbering, nonzeros lie in a narrow band around the main diagonal.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Convention):** Each Part B question below is internally divided into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks, totaling 14 marks. **Module Internal Choice** is provided: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**A three-bar planar truss** is shown in the figure (treat as schematic — bar 1 from node 1 to node 2, bar 2 from node 2 to node 3, bar 3 from node 1 to node 3, forming a triangle). Each bar has axial rigidity $EA = $ constant. Nodes 1 and 2 are pinned. A horizontal load $P$ is applied at node 3.

**(a)** Derive the element stiffness matrix for a 2D truss bar in global coordinates. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Assemble the global stiffness matrix for the truss using the direct stiffness method and write the equilibrium equation. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

For a 2D truss bar of length $L$ making angle $\theta$ with the global $X$-axis, the element stiffness matrix in global coordinates is:

$$
[k]^e = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c = \cos\theta$ and $s = \sin\theta$.

**Derivation (key steps):**

1. Local stiffness: $[k]^e_{\text{local}} = \dfrac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ with DOFs $(u_i', v_i', u_j', v_j')$ in local axis $x'$.
2. Displacement transformation: $\begin{Bmatrix} u_i' \\ v_i' \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c & s \\ -s & c \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_i \\ v_i \end{Bmatrix}$.
3. Stacking: $[T]^e = \begin{bmatrix} c & s & 0 & 0 \\ -s & c & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & c & s \\ 0 & 0 & -s & c \end{bmatrix}$.
4. Global stiffness: $[k]^e_{\text{global}} = [T]^e{}^T [k]^e_{\text{local}} [T]^e$, which yields the $4 \times 4$ matrix above.

#### Model Solution (b)

Label the global DOFs as: $\{U_1, V_1, U_2, V_2, U_3, V_3\}$ — total $N = 6$.

**Location matrices:**

- Bar 1 (nodes 1 → 2): $\Delta^1 = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}^T$
- Bar 2 (nodes 2 → 3): $\Delta^2 = \{3, 4, 5, 6\}^T$
- Bar 3 (nodes 1 → 3): $\Delta^3 = \{1, 2, 5, 6\}^T$

**Assembly (exhaustive):**

For each element, scatter its $4 \times 4$ block into the corresponding rows/columns of the global $6 \times 6$ matrix using the LM.

The assembled global matrix (assuming the specific geometry of the triangle with three bar angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$) takes the block form:

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} K_{11} & K_{12} & K_{13} \\ K_{12}^T & K_{22} & K_{23} \\ K_{13}^T & K_{23}^T & K_{33} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where each $K_{IJ}$ is a $2 \times 2$ submatrix and

$$
K_{11} = \frac{EA}{L_1} \begin{bmatrix} c_1^2 & c_1 s_1 \\ c_1 s_1 & s_1^2 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{EA}{L_3} \begin{bmatrix} c_3^2 & c_3 s_3 \\ c_3 s_3 & s_3^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

(and similarly for the remaining $2 \times 2$ blocks obtained by scattering the relevant element matrices).

**Global load vector:**

$$
\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_{1x} \\ F_{1y} \\ F_{2x} \\ F_{2y} \\ P \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Equilibrium equation:**

$$
[K]_{6 \times 6} \, \{U_1, V_1, U_2, V_2, U_3, V_3\}^T = \{F\}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** A common mistake is to forget that the global matrix is $6 \times 6$ for a 2D truss with 3 nodes (each node has 2 DOFs). Students often produce a $3 \times 3$ matrix as if it were a 1D problem — this costs 2-3 marks immediately.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A 1D stepped bar** is fixed at node 1 and free at node 4, with three elements of stiffnesses $k_1$, $k_2$, $k_3$ in series. A load $P$ is applied at node 4.

**(a)** Derive the assembled global stiffness matrix $[K]$ and global load vector $\{F\}$ for the bar, using the direct stiffness method. State the location matrix for each element. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Apply the boundary condition $u_1 = 0$ and solve for the nodal displacements $u_2$, $u_3$, $u_4$ in terms of $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and $P$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Element stiffness matrices:**

$$
[k]^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 \\ -k_1 & k_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} k_2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix} k_3 & -k_3 \\ -k_3 & k_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Location matrices:**

- Element 1 (nodes 1 → 2): $\Delta^1 = \{1, 2\}^T$
- Element 2 (nodes 2 → 3): $\Delta^2 = \{2, 3\}^T$
- Element 3 (nodes 3 → 4): $\Delta^3 = \{3, 4\}^T$

**Step 3 — Assembly (scatter add):**

Initialize $[K]_{4 \times 4} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\{F\}_{4 \times 1} = \mathbf{0}$.

- From element 1: $K_{1,1} += k_1$, $K_{1,2} += -k_1$, $K_{2,1} += -k_1$, $K_{2,2} += k_1$
- From element 2: $K_{2,2} += k_2$, $K_{2,3} += -k_2$, $K_{3,2} += -k_2$, $K_{3,3} += k_2$
- From element 3: $K_{3,3} += k_3$, $K_{3,4} += -k_3$, $K_{4,3} += -k_3$, $K_{4,4} += k_3$

**Step 4 — Assembled global matrix:**

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1+k_2 & -k_2 & 0 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2+k_3 & -k_3 \\ 0 & 0 & -k_3 & k_3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Global load vector:**

$$
\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Apply $u_1 = 0$.** Eliminate row 1 and column 1:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} k_1+k_2 & -k_2 & 0 \\ -k_2 & k_2+k_3 & -k_3 \\ 0 & -k_3 & k_3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \\ u_4 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve by back-substitution (analytical pattern).**

From the last row: $k_3(u_4 - u_3) = P$, hence $u_4 - u_3 = P / k_3$.

From the second row: $k_2(u_3 - u_2) + k_3(u_3 - u_4) = 0$, hence $u_3 - u_2 = P/k_3$ as well (i.e., the element 2 internal force equals the element 3 force).

From the first row: $k_1 u_2 + k_2(u_2 - u_3) = 0$, hence $u_2 = (P/k_3) \cdot k_2 / (k_1 + k_2) = \dfrac{P k_2}{k_3 (k_1 + k_2)}$.

**Step 3 — Final results:**

$$
u_2 = \frac{P k_2}{k_3 (k_1 + k_2)}
$$

$$
u_3 = u_2 + \frac{P}{k_3} = \frac{P k_2}{k_3 (k_1 + k_2)} + \frac{P}{k_3} = \frac{P}{k_3} \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1+k_2} + 1 \right) = \frac{P}{k_3} \cdot \frac{k_1 + 2k_2}{k_1 + k_2}
$$

$$
u_4 = u_3 + \frac{P}{k_3} = \frac{P}{k_3} \cdot \frac{k_1 + 2k_2}{k_1 + k_2} + \frac{P}{k_3} = \frac{P}{k_3} \cdot \frac{k_1 + 2k_2 + (k_1 + k_2)}{k_1 + k_2} = \frac{P}{k_3} \cdot \frac{2k_1 + 3k_2}{k_1 + k_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (equivalent stiffness method):**

For springs in series:

$$
u_4 = \frac{P}{k_{\text{eq}}} = P \left( \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2} + \frac{1}{k_3} \right)
$$

Substituting the closed-form $u_4$ from Step 3 should reproduce this identity. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Students frequently forget to apply the BC *after* assembly. The correct sequence is: **(i) assemble first, (ii) apply BCs second, (iii) solve last.** Many students apply BCs to the element matrix, which is conceptually wrong because the boundary condition is on a *global* DOF, not a local one.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Assembly = summation of element contributions into a global system.**
- [x] **The location matrix $\Delta^e$ (or LM) is the master map** that converts local DOF indices to global DOF indices.
- [x] **Rule:** $K_{\Delta^e_i, \, \Delta^e_j} = K_{\Delta^e_i, \, \Delta^e_j} + k^e_{i,j}$ — additive scatter.
- [x] **Rule (force):** $F_{\Delta^e_i} = F_{\Delta^e_i} + f^e_i$ — additive scatter.
- [x] **Global $[K]$ is symmetric, sparse, banded, and singular (before BCs).**
- [x] **Bandwidth depends on numbering**, not on the physical structure. Good numbering ⇒ narrow bandwidth ⇒ fast solve.
- [x] **BCs are applied *after* assembly**, not before. The penalty method uses $K_{ii} \to \alpha, F_i \to \alpha u_i$.
- [x] **Element matrices are derived in local coords**, transformed to global via $[T]^e$, then assembled.
- [x] **Total global DOF** $N$ = (DOFs per node) $\times$ (number of nodes) — for 2D truss, $N = 2 \times$ (nodes).
- [x] **For 1D bar elements**, each element contributes a $2 \times 2$ block; for 2D truss, $4 \times 4$; for 2D quad, $8 \times 8$.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting the **transformation** from local to global before assembly. The element matrix *must* be in global coordinates before being scattered into $[K]$.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** Using a *non-zero* element DOF index that does not match the location matrix — the global matrix will have wrong entries.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** Computing $\det([K])$ *before* BCs and getting 0, then panicking. The matrix is *supposed* to be singular at that stage.
- [x] **Direct Stiffness Method = Local solve + Assemble + Global solve** — a three-step mantra for viva.
- [x] **Assembly cost is $O(E \cdot n_e^2)$** — scales linearly with mesh count for fixed $n_e$.
- [x] **Skyline storage** is used for variable-bandwidth matrices; **CSR/CSC** for fully sparse matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Stiffness Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Stiffness Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — PROPERTIES OF STIFFNESS MATRIX

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **stiffness matrix** (also called the **global stiffness matrix**, denoted as $[K]$) is a symmetric, square, banded matrix that maps nodal displacements to nodal forces in a discretized finite element system. The fundamental finite element equilibrium equation is:

$$
[K]\{d\} = \{F\}
$$

where $\{d\}$ is the global nodal displacement vector and $\{F\}$ is the global nodal force vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The properties of the global stiffness matrix are foundational to Module 1. Questions frequently appear in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) forms. Mastering these properties is critical before tackling 1D and 2D element derivations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spring Network"

Imagine a child's construction set where rigid rods are connected at joints with **rubber bands (springs)**. Pulling one joint (applying a **displacement**) causes forces to develop at *all* connected joints. The **stiffness matrix** is simply a numerical "translator" that:

- **Tells you** how much force appears at every joint when you pull just one joint.
- **Stores** all the spring stiffnesses between every pair of connected nodes.
- **Diagonal entry $K_{ii}$** = total stiffness resisting motion of node $i$ in a particular DOF.
- **Off-diagonal entry $K_{ij}$** = coupling stiffness — how much force appears at node $i$ when node $j$ is displaced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of units:** For structural problems, $[K]$ has units of **N/m** (Force/Length). For thermal problems, units become **W/(°C)**. For fluid flow, units are **m³/s · s/kg** depending on formulation.

### 1.3 Source of the Stiffness Matrix

The stiffness matrix for an individual element is obtained by evaluating:

$$
[k] = \int_{V} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV
$$

where:
- $[B]$ = strain-displacement matrix (derivative of shape functions)
- $[D]$ = material property matrix (constitutive matrix)
- $V$ = volume of the element

The **global stiffness matrix** is then assembled by summing elemental contributions using the **Direct Stiffness Method**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive List of Stiffness Matrix Properties

The global stiffness matrix $[K]$ exhibits the following fundamental properties:

### Property 1: Symmetry
$[K]$ is a **symmetric matrix**, i.e., $[K] = [K]^T$, or equivalently, $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$.

**Why?** This arises because:
- The strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2}\{d\}^T[K]\{d\}$ is a **scalar quadratic form** in displacements.
- For a unique scalar, the matrix must be symmetric.
- Mathematically, the bilinear operator $\int_V B_i D_{ij} B_j \, dV$ is symmetric in node indices.

### Property 2: Singular Nature
For an unconstrained (free) structure, $\det([K]) = 0$. Equivalently, $[K]^{-1}$ does **not exist** in the unconstrained case.

**Why?** A free rigid body has 3 zero-energy deformation modes (2 in 2D, 3 in 3D) corresponding to **rigid body motions** that produce zero strain.

### Property 3: Positive Semi-Definiteness
For an unconstrained structure, $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} \geq 0$ for all real vectors $\{d\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once **essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions** are applied, $[K]$ becomes **positive definite** — meaning $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} > 0$ for all non-zero $\{d\}$, and the system has a **unique solution**.

### Property 4: Banded & Sparse Structure
$[K]$ is a **banded matrix** with non-zero entries confined near the **main diagonal**. The **half-bandwidth** $B$ is given by:

$$
B = (D + 1) \times q
$$

where:
- $D$ = maximum difference in node numbers connected to any single element (max nodal gap)
- $q$ = number of DOFs per node

### Property 5: Diagonal Dominance
Each diagonal element is greater than or equal to the sum of absolute values of off-diagonal elements in its row:

$$
K_{ii} \geq \sum_{j \neq i} \vert K_{ij} \vert
$$

with equality for unconstrained DOFs and strict inequality for constrained DOFs.

### Property 6: Non-Negative Diagonal Entries
$K_{ii} \geq 0$ for all $i$.

### Property 7: Off-Diagonal Entries Non-Positive
$K_{ij} \leq 0$ for $i \neq j$ (this is true for spring/structural systems).

### Property 8: Singularly Perturbed by Boundary Conditions
Applying a constraint at DOF $i$ eliminates row $i$ and column $i$ (or sets the corresponding $K_{ii} = 1$ in the penalty method).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Engineering Significance** |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Equilibrium equation | $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$ | Core FEA relation |
| Element stiffness | $[k]^e = \int_V [B]^T[D][B] \, dV$ | Elemental formulation |
| Strain energy (symmetric proof) | $U = \frac{1}{2}\{d\}^T[K]\{d\}$ | Requires $[K] = [K]^T$ |
| Singularity | $\det([K]) = 0$ (free body) | Need BCs to solve |
| Positive definiteness (with BCs) | $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} > 0$ | Unique solution |
| Half-bandwidth | $B = (D+1) \cdot q$ | Storage efficiency |
| Diagonal dominance | $K_{ii} \geq \sum_{j\neq i} \vert K_{ij} \vert$ | Convergence of iterative solvers |
| Total DOF count | $N = (\text{nodes}) \times q$ | Size of $[K]$ |
| Max rigid body modes | 3 (3D), 2 (2D), 1 (1D) | Free-body eigenvalues |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - **Structural analysis software** (ANSYS, Abaqus, NASTRAN) exploits symmetry to halve memory usage.
> - **Sparse solvers** (e.g., skyline, frontal, multifrontal) exploit banding to solve million-DOF problems.
> - **Iterative solvers** (CG, GMRES) rely on positive definiteness for guaranteed convergence.
> - **Engineering judgment**: A zero pivot during solution means a rigid body mode is unconstrained — a real safety check.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Proof of Symmetry

**Starting point** — The strain energy stored in an elastic body is:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \{\varepsilon\}^T \{\sigma\} \, dV
$$

Substituting the constitutive relation $\{\sigma\} = [D]\{\varepsilon\}$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \{\varepsilon\}^T [D] \{\varepsilon\} \, dV
$$

Substituting the strain-displacement relation $\{\varepsilon\} = [B]\{d\}^e$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \int_V (\{d\}^e)^T [B]^T [D] [B] \{d\}^e \, dV
$$

Since $\{d\}^e$ is constant with respect to the integration variable:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} (\{d\}^e)^T \left[ \int_V [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right] \{d\}^e
$$

Recognizing the bracketed term as the **element stiffness matrix**:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} (\{d\}^e)^T [k]^e \{d\}^e
$$

For the strain energy $U$ to be a **scalar** (a single number), the matrix $[k]^e$ must be symmetric. We prove this rigorously:

$$
[k]^e = \int_V [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV
$$

Taking the transpose:

$$
([k]^e)^T = \int_V ([B]^T [D] [B])^T \, dV
$$

Using the transpose property $(ABC)^T = C^T B^T A^T$:

$$
([k]^e)^T = \int_V [B]^T [D]^T [B] \, dV
$$

For an elastic, non-dissipative material, the constitutive matrix is **symmetric**: $[D]^T = [D]$. Therefore:

$$
([k]^e)^T = \int_V [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV = [k]^e
$$

$$
\boxed{[k]^e = ([k]^e)^T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Symmetry proved}}
$$

The same argument applies to the global stiffness matrix $[K]$ after assembly.

## 3.2 Detailed Proof of Singular Nature (Unconstrained)

Consider an **eigenvalue problem** for the stiffness matrix:

$$
[K]\{\phi\} = \lambda [M]\{\phi\}
$$

For a **rigid body mode** $\{\phi_r\}$, the strain energy must be zero:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \{\phi_r\}^T [K] \{\phi_r\} = 0
$$

Since $\{\phi_r\}^T [K] \{\phi_r\} = 0$ for non-zero $\{\phi_r\}$, the matrix $[K]$ has a **zero eigenvalue** ($\lambda = 0$). A matrix with at least one zero eigenvalue has:

$$
\det([K]) = 0
$$

Therefore, $[K]$ is **singular** and its inverse does not exist in the unconstrained state.

**Worked Example — 2-Node Truss Element:**

For a 1D bar element of length $L$, area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$:

$$
[k]^e = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The determinant is:

$$
\det([k]^e) = \left(\frac{AE}{L}\right)^2 (1 \cdot 1 - (-1)(-1)) = \left(\frac{AE}{L}\right)^2 (0) = 0
$$

This confirms **singularity** — a single free bar element can translate as a rigid body without strain.

## 3.3 Detailed Proof of Positive Semi-Definiteness

The strain energy for *any* displacement field is non-negative:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \{d\}^T [K] \{d\} \geq 0
$$

This is because strain energy is physically always $\geq 0$ (an elastic body cannot store negative energy). Hence:

$$
\{d\}^T [K] \{d\} \geq 0 \quad \forall \text{ real } \{d\}
$$

This is the **definition of positive semi-definiteness**.

**With applied boundary conditions**, the constrained displacement vector $\{d\}$ cannot include rigid body modes. Hence the only way to get $U = 0$ is $\{d\} = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\{d\}^T [K] \{d\} > 0 \quad \forall \{d\} \neq 0
$$

This is **strict positive definiteness**, guaranteeing a unique solution to $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$.

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation of Half-Bandwidth

Consider an element connecting node $i$ and node $i + m$ (where $m$ is the nodal gap). The number of non-zero off-diagonal entries in this row is determined by the largest $|i - j|$ over all connected node pairs.

For a general element with maximum node difference $D = i_{max} - i_{min}$:

- The number of nodes between $i_{min}$ and $i_{max}$ inclusive is $D + 1$.
- Each node contributes $q$ DOFs.
- Therefore, the **half-bandwidth** in rows is:

$$
\boxed{B = (D + 1) \times q}
$$

**Worked Example:** A 4-node quadrilateral with DOFs $u_x, u_y$ per node, max nodal gap $D = 3$:
$$
B = (3 + 1) \times 2 = 8
$$

This means non-zero entries are confined to a band of 8 columns on either side of the diagonal.

## 3.5 Worked Problem — Constructing $[K]$ for a 2-Bar Truss

**Problem:** Two bar elements connected at node 2, with node 1 and node 3 fixed.

$$
[k]_1 = \frac{A_1 E_1}{L_1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]_2 = \frac{A_2 E_2}{L_2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

For simplicity, let $k_1 = k_2 = k$ (equal stiffness). The global matrix before applying BCs is:

$$
[K] = k \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Apply boundary conditions** $d_1 = d_3 = 0$ (eliminate rows/columns 1 and 3):

$$
[K]_{reduced} = [2k] \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = 2k \, d_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_2 = \frac{F_2}{2k}
$$

**Verification of properties:**
- Symmetric: $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k$ ✓
- Before BCs: $\det([K]) = 0$ (singular) ✓
- After BCs: $\det([2k]) = 2k > 0$ (positive definite) ✓
- Banded: non-zero entries only adjacent to diagonal ✓
- Diagonal dominance: $|2k| \geq |-1| + |-1| = 2k$ ✓ (equality holds for unconstrained interior node — strict only at constrained DOFs)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stiffness Matrix Properties Map

    B --> B3[Positive Semi-Definite]

    C --> C1[Real Valued Entries]

    D --> D1[Banded Structure]

    B1 --> E[Source: Energy is Scalar]

        M --> R5[Row 5: K54, K55, K56, K57]

    R1 -.-> Z1[Zeros outside band]
    R2 -.-> Z1
    R3 -.-> Z1
    R4 -.-> Z1
    R5 -.-> Z1
    
    style M fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
