
---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State and explain any three properties of the stiffness matrix in the finite element method."**

**Model Answer:**

The stiffness matrix $[K]$ in FEA has the following key properties:

1. **Symmetry:** $[K] = [K]^T$, i.e., $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$. This arises because the strain energy expression $U = \frac{1}{2}\{d\}^T[K]\{d\}$ must yield a scalar value, requiring the matrix to be symmetric. It also follows from the symmetry of the material constitutive matrix $[D]$.

2. **Singularity (unconstrained):** For a structure without boundary conditions, $\det([K]) = 0$. This corresponds to the existence of zero-energy rigid body modes. Applying essential boundary conditions converts $[K]$ to a non-singular, positive-definite matrix.

3. **Positive Semi-Definiteness:** $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} \geq 0$ for all real displacement vectors, reflecting the non-negative nature of strain energy. With applied boundary conditions, $[K]$ becomes strictly positive definite, ensuring a unique solution.

4. **Banded and Sparse:** Non-zero entries are confined to a band around the main diagonal, with half-bandwidth $B = (D+1)q$, enabling efficient storage and computation.

5. **Diagonal Dominance:** The diagonal entry satisfies $K_{ii} \geq \sum_{j \neq i} \vert K_{ij} \vert$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is the global stiffness matrix singular in the absence of boundary conditions? How is this issue resolved?"**

**Model Answer:**

The global stiffness matrix $[K]$ is singular when no boundary conditions are applied because the **free (unconstrained) structure can undergo rigid body motions** without developing any internal strain. These rigid body modes correspond to **zero eigenvalues** of $[K]$, mathematically implying $\det([K]) = 0$.

For a 3D body, there are **3 rigid body translation modes**; for 2D problems, there are **3 modes** (2 translations + 1 rotation); and for 1D problems, there is **1 translation mode**.

**Resolution:** This singularity is resolved by applying **essential (Dirichlet) boundary conditions**, which prescribe known displacements (often zero) at specific nodes. This eliminates the rigid body modes, reduces the system size, and converts $[K]$ to a **positive definite** matrix with a unique solution. The modified system is:

$$
[K_{reduced}]\{d_{unknown}\} = \{F_{reduced}\}
$$

where $[K_{reduced}]$ is non-singular and invertible.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the element stiffness matrix of a 1D bar element using the principle of minimum potential energy. Show that the resulting matrix is symmetric and singular for a single free element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-bar truss system with elements 1 and 2 connected at node 2, with $k_1 = k_2 = k$, construct the global stiffness matrix. Verify all key properties (symmetry, singularity, banding, diagonal dominance). Then apply boundary conditions at nodes 1 and 3 and solve for the displacement at node 2 if $F_2 = 10$ kN and $k = 5$ kN/mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Element stiffness matrix of 1D bar using minimum potential energy:**

The total potential energy $\Pi$ is:

$$
\Pi = U - W_{ext}
$$

where $U$ is strain energy and $W_{ext}$ is work done by external forces.

For a 1D bar with linear shape functions $N_1 = 1 - x/L$, $N_2 = x/L$:

The strain-displacement matrix is:

$$
[B] = \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The element stiffness matrix is:

$$
[k]^e = \int_V [B]^T[D][B] \, dV = \int_0^L \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} (AE) \frac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx
$$

$$
[k]^e = \frac{AE}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Symmetry verification:** $K_{12} = -AE/L = K_{21}$ ✓

**Singularity verification:** $\det([k]^e) = (AE/L)^2 (1 - 1) = 0$ ✓

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Global stiffness matrix assembly:**

The element matrices are:

$$
[k]_1 = k\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [k]_2 = k\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Global matrix (3×3):

$$
[K] = k\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Property verification:**
- **Symmetry:** $K_{12} = K_{21} = -k$, $K_{13} = K_{31} = 0$, $K_{23} = K_{32} = -k$ ✓
- **Singularity:** $\det([K]) = k^3(1(2) - (-1)(-1)) = k^3(0) = 0$ ✓
- **Banded:** Half-bandwidth $B = 1$ (since max node gap = 1, $q=1$, $B = (1+1) \times 1 = 2$, but in 1D this gives 2 entries per row)
- **Diagonal dominance:** Row 2: $|2k| = 2k = |-k| + |-k| = 2k$ ✓ (equality holds for unconstrained interior node)

**Apply BCs** $d_1 = d_3 = 0$:

$$
K_{reduced} = [2k] = [10] \text{ kN/mm}
$$

**Solve:** $10 \cdot d_2 = 10 \Rightarrow d_2 = 1$ mm

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the diagonal dominance property of the stiffness matrix. Show that for a 3-node 1D system with equal stiffness $k$, the global matrix satisfies diagonal dominance at all nodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the banded nature of the global stiffness matrix. For a mesh with maximum node number difference $D$ in any element and $q$ DOFs per node, derive the half-bandwidth formula. Illustrate with an example where $D = 4$ and $q = 3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Diagonal Dominance Explanation:**

A matrix $[K]$ is **diagonally dominant** if:

$$
K_{ii} \geq \sum_{j \neq i} \vert K_{ij} \vert
$$

For constrained DOFs, the inequality is strict; for unconstrained DOFs, equality often holds.

**Physical interpretation:** The stiffness resisting motion of node $i$ is at least as large as the sum of all coupling stiffnesses connecting node $i$ to other nodes. This ensures the matrix is well-conditioned and iterative solvers converge.

**3-Node System Example:**

For nodes 1, 2, 3 with elements (1-2) and (2-3), each with stiffness $k$:

$$
[K] = k\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Row 1:** $K_{11} = k$, $\sum |K_{1j}| = |-k| + |0| = k$ → $k \geq k$ ✓ (equality)
**Row 2:** $K_{22} = 2k$, $\sum |K_{2j}| = |-k| + |-k| = 2k$ → $2k \geq 2k$ ✓ (equality)
**Row 3:** $K_{33} = k$, $\sum |K_{3j}| = |0| + |-k| = k$ → $k \geq k$ ✓ (equality)

All nodes satisfy diagonal dominance. After applying BCs at nodes 1 and 3, the reduced system has strict inequality.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Banded Nature Discussion:**

The global stiffness matrix is **banded** because element connectivity is local — each element connects only a few nearby nodes. Consequently, $[K]_{ij} \neq 0$ only if nodes $i$ and $j$ belong to the same element.

**Half-Bandwidth Derivation:**

Consider an element spanning nodes from $n_{min}$ to $n_{max}$ with difference $D = n_{max} - n_{min}$. This element contributes non-zero entries to rows $n_{min}$ through $n_{max}$ and columns $n_{min}$ through $n_{max}$.

The band extends $D$ positions on either side of the diagonal. With $q$ DOFs per node:

$$
\boxed{B = (D + 1) \times q}
$$

**Example:** $D = 4$, $q = 3$:

$$
B = (4 + 1) \times 3 = 15
$$

This means non-zero entries exist within 15 positions of the main diagonal (i.e., 7 or 8 columns on each side, depending on convention). This sparsity enables efficient storage using banded or skyline storage schemes, reducing memory from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \cdot B)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing positive semi-definite with positive definite** — A free stiffness matrix is semi-definite; only after BCs does it become definite. Marks lost if this distinction is missed.
> 2. **Forgetting the DOF factor in bandwidth** — Students often write $B = D + 1$ instead of $B = (D+1) \times q$. Always multiply by DOFs per node.
> 3. **Claiming $[K]$ is always singular** — It is singular *only* in the unconstrained state. After applying essential BCs, it becomes non-singular.
> 4. **Missing the proof of symmetry** — Just stating $[K] = [K]^T$ is incomplete; you must show it follows from the symmetry of $[D]$ and the strain energy formulation.
> 5. **Sign errors in off-diagonal terms** — Off-diagonal entries in structural stiffness matrices are typically **non-positive** (negative or zero), not positive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Stiffness matrix definition:** $[K]\{d\} = \{F\}$, obtained from $[k]^e = \int_V [B]^T[D][B]\,dV$
- **Symmetry:** $[K] = [K]^T$ (proved via strain energy scalar argument + $[D] = [D]^T$)
- **Singularity:** $\det([K]) = 0$ for unconstrained systems (rigid body modes cause zero eigenvalues)
- **Positive semi-definiteness (free):** $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} \geq 0$
- **Positive definiteness (constrained):** $\{d\}^T[K]\{d\} > 0$ for $\{d\} \neq 0$
- **Banded structure:** Half-bandwidth $B = (D + 1) \times q$
- **Diagonal dominance:** $K_{ii} \geq \sum_{j \neq i} \vert K_{ij} \vert$
- **Non-negative diagonal:** $K_{ii} \geq 0$
- **Non-positive off-diagonal:** $K_{ij} \leq 0$ for $i \neq j$ (structural systems)
- **Sparse storage:** Only $O(N \cdot B)$ storage needed, not $O(N^2)$
- **Rigid body modes:** 3 in 3D, 3 in 2D, 1 in 1D
- **Boundary conditions:** Essential (Dirichlet) BCs make $[K]$ non-singular and positive definite
- **Element assembly:** Global $[K] = \sum_e A_e^T [k]^e A_e$ (assembly operator)
- **Solution methods:** Cholesky (for symmetric positive definite), LU, Conjugate Gradient
- **Energy interpretation:** $U = \frac{1}{2}\{d\}^T[K]\{d\}$ must be non-negative

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "state properties," always provide both the **mathematical statement** and the **physical/engineering reason**. KTU examiners award marks for understanding, not just memorization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment of Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Treatment of Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Finite Element Method (FEM)**, the term **Boundary Conditions (BCs)** refers to the prescribed values of the field variables (primary variables) and/or their derivatives (secondary variables) that must be satisfied at the boundaries of the problem domain to obtain a physically meaningful and mathematically unique solution to the governing differential equation.

Mathematically, for a generic boundary $\Gamma$ of a domain $\Omega$, the boundary conditions are classified into two fundamental categories:

$$\Gamma = \Gamma_u \cup \Gamma_q, \quad \Gamma_u \cap \Gamma_q = \emptyset$$

where $\Gamma_u$ denotes the **Essential (Dirichlet) boundary** and $\Gamma_q$ denotes the **Natural (Neumann) boundary**.

| Boundary Type | Physical Meaning (1-D Bar) | Mathematical Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Essential BC** (Primary) | Prescribed displacement $u$ | $u(x) = \bar{u}$ on $\Gamma_u$ |
| **Natural BC** (Secondary) | Prescribed traction/force $P$ | $EA \frac{du}{dx} = \bar{P}$ on $\Gamma_q$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the stiffness matrix approach, the *essential* BCs are called **Geometric Boundary Conditions** because they constrain the geometric (primary) variable, while *natural* BCs are called **Force Boundary Conditions** because they prescribe the forces/fluxes (secondary variable). The governing equilibrium equation is always written for the unconstrained system; BCs are then **imposed afterwards** as a post-assembly step.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are solving a system of linear equations $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$ that represents a 3-storey building under unknown lateral loads. The system is initially *singular* or *non-unique* because pure equilibrium gives you only relative displacements. The building could sway freely like a balloon.

- **Essential BC (Geometric BC):** This is like **nailing the base of the building to the ground**. By fixing the ground floor displacement to zero ($u_1 = 0$), you remove that unknown entirely. The building now has a definite, absolute position in space.
- **Natural BC (Force BC):** This is like **applying a known wind load of 50 kN at the top floor**. You specify the force (the RHS) without restricting the displacement.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Takeaway:** Essential BCs modify the **structure of the stiffness matrix** (geometry), while Natural BCs simply modify the **load vector** (force). In KTU-style problems, you will almost always be asked to apply essential BCs after assembling the global stiffness matrix and global load vector.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

For structural problems in the KTU 2024 PEPET632 syllabus, the standard metric is **force per unit displacement (kN/mm or N/m)** for the stiffness matrix entries. The convergence criteria for numerical methods typically use a tolerance of **$\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$** when iterating penalty parameters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Boundary Condition Treatment Methods

After assembling the global finite element equation:

$$[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$$

where $[K]$ is the global stiffness matrix, $\{U\}$ is the nodal displacement vector, and $\{F\}$ is the global force vector, the system is generally **ill-conditioned** or even **singular** because rigid body motions have not been suppressed. The following methods are used to impose BCs:

### 2.1.1 The Direct Method (Elimination / One-Shot Method)

This is the **most commonly tested** method in KTU board examinations.

1. **Partition** the global displacement vector into **prescribed (constrained)** and **free (unknown)** parts: $\{U\} = \begin{Bmatrix} U_f \\ U_p \end{Bmatrix}$.
2. **Similarly partition** the stiffness matrix and force vector.
3. The partitioned system is:
$$\begin{bmatrix} K_{ff} & K_{fp} \\ K_{pf} & K_{pp} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} U_f \\ U_p \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_f \\ F_p \end{Bmatrix}$$
4. **Eliminate** the prescribed DOFs by removing the corresponding rows and columns, yielding a **reduced system** that is solved for $\{U_f\}$.
5. **Back-substitute** to recover the nodal reactions at the prescribed DOFs using $\{F_p\} = [K_{pf}]\{U_f\} + [K_{pp}]\{U_p\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Removing a row/column is mathematically equivalent to setting the corresponding displacement to a known constant, which collapses the system into a smaller, non-singular matrix. The penalty is a slightly altered bandwidth pattern.

### 2.1.2 The Penalty Method

Instead of physically deleting rows and columns, we **add a very large number** $\alpha$ (typically $10^6$ to $10^{15}$ times the largest diagonal entry) to the diagonal terms corresponding to prescribed DOFs.

$$([K] + [P])\{U\} = \{F\} + [P]\{U_p\}$$

where $[P] = \alpha [I_p]$ is the penalty matrix, $[I_p]$ being an identity matrix with ones only at prescribed DOF positions.

1. The system is solved in **one shot** without partitioning.
2. The prescribed displacement is **approximately enforced**: the solution converges to the exact value as $\alpha \to \infty$.
3. The penalty $\alpha$ must be **finite but large**; if $\alpha$ is too small, BCs are violated, and if $\alpha$ is too large, the matrix becomes **ill-conditioned**.

### 2.1.3 The Partition Method

A variant of the Direct Method where the reduced system is explicitly written as:

$$[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} = \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$$

This is the **direct working equation** that KTU expects you to write down. The RHS combines the external load on free DOFs $\{F_f\}$ with the *load transferred* from prescribed DOFs $-[K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$.

### 2.1.4 The Lagrange Multiplier Method

A mathematically rigorous approach where additional unknowns $\lambda$ (Lagrange multipliers) are introduced at prescribed DOFs, augmenting the global system with constraint equations. This method produces a **saddle-point matrix** but preserves sparsity and is rarely required at the KTU board level.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your exam-day ready reference.** Memorize the symbols and the structure of the equations.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Units (SI) | Constraint Category |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $[K]$ | Global Stiffness Matrix | $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{kN/mm}$ | System matrix |
| $\{U\}$ | Global Nodal Displacement Vector | $\text{m}$ or $\text{mm}$ | Primary variable |
| $\{F\}$ | Global Force Vector | $\text{N}$ or $\text{kN}$ | Secondary variable |
| $\alpha$ | Penalty Parameter | Dimensionless scalar | $\alpha \gg \max \vert K_{ii} \vert$ |
| $\Gamma_u$ | Essential (Geometric) Boundary | Length | $u = \bar{u}$ prescribed |
| $\Gamma_q$ | Natural (Force) Boundary | Length | $q = \bar{q}$ prescribed |
| $\{U_f\}$ | Free DOF displacement vector | $\text{m}$ | Solved via $[K_{ff}]^{-1}$ |
| $\{U_p\}$ | Prescribed DOF displacement vector | $\text{m}$ | Known a priori |
| $[K_{fp}]$ | Coupling sub-matrix | $\text{N/m}$ | Cross-coupling stiffness |
| $\lambda$ | Lagrange Multiplier | $\text{N}$ | Reaction force |

**Master Equation — Partition Method:**
$$[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} = \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$$

**Master Equation — Penalty Method:**
$$([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} = \{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In aerospace and civil engineering, boundary condition treatment is critical for:
- **Fixed supports in bridges:** Essential BC at the abutment (zero vertical displacement).
- **Pressure vessels:** Natural BC (traction) on the inner surface.
- **Heat transfer in turbine blades:** Essential BC for temperature at the cooling channels; natural BC for convective flux on the outer surface.
- **Modal analysis:** Incorrect BC treatment leads to spurious **zero-frequency modes** (rigid body modes) that contaminate the eigenvalue spectrum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Partition Method (Reduced System)

We begin with the assembled global finite element equation of a discretized structure containing $n$ total degrees of freedom (DOFs).

$$[K]_{n \times n} \{U\}_{n \times 1} = \{F\}_{n \times 1}$$

**Step 1 — Decompose the displacement vector.**
Suppose the first $p$ DOFs are prescribed (constrained) and the remaining $f = n - p$ DOFs are free. We re-order the vector $\{U\}$ as:

$$\{U\} = \begin{Bmatrix} U_f \\ U_p \end{Bmatrix}$$

The vector $\{U_p\}$ is **known** from the essential boundary conditions, while $\{U_f\}$ is **unknown**.

**Step 2 — Re-order the stiffness matrix and force vector accordingly.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} K_{ff} & K_{fp} \\ K_{pf} & K_{pp} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} U_f \\ U_p \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} F_f \\ F_p \end{Bmatrix}$$

where:
- $[K_{ff}]_{f \times f}$ : stiffness sub-matrix coupling free DOFs to free DOFs.
- $[K_{fp}]_{f \times p}$ : stiffness sub-matrix coupling free DOFs to prescribed DOFs.
- $[K_{pf}] = [K_{fp}]^T$ : stiffness sub-matrix coupling prescribed DOFs to free DOFs (by symmetry).
- $[K_{pp}]_{p \times p}$ : stiffness sub-matrix coupling prescribed DOFs to prescribed DOFs.

**Step 3 — Perform matrix multiplication of the first block row.**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} + [K_{fp}]\{U_p\} &= \{F_f\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the unknown vector $\{U_f\}$.**

Move the coupling term to the right-hand side:

$$\begin{aligned}
[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} &= \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental working equation of the Partition Method**. Note that $\{U_p\}$ is known, so $\{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$ is a known vector.

**Step 5 — Solve for free DOFs by matrix inversion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\{U_f\} &= [K_{ff}]^{-1} \left( \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Back-substitute to recover the reaction forces.**

From the second block row:
$$\begin{aligned}
\{F_p\} &= [K_{pf}]\{U_f\} + [K_{pp}]\{U_p\}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\{U_f\}$ has just been computed, $\{F_p\}$ is now fully determined. Physically, $\{F_p\}$ represents the **support reactions** at the prescribed DOFs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Penalty Method

Consider the global equation $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$. We wish to enforce $u_i = \bar{u}_i$ at prescribed DOFs without deleting rows.

**Step 1 — Construct the penalty matrix.**
Define a diagonal penalty matrix $[P]$:

$$[P] = \alpha \begin{bmatrix} 0 & & & & \\ & \ddots & & & \\ & & 1 & & \\ & & & \ddots & \\ & & & & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The "1" sits at the $i$-th prescribed DOF; all other entries are zero.

**Step 2 — Add the penalty matrix to the original system.**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K]\{U\} + [P]\{U\} &= \{F\} + [P]\{U_p\}
\end{aligned}$$

Note that $[P]\{U\} = \alpha[I_p]\{U\}$ adds $\alpha u_i$ to the $i$-th row of the LHS.

**Step 3 — Rearrange the augmented equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} &= \{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify enforcement of the BC.**
Look at the $i$-th row of the augmented system. The LHS becomes $K_{i1}u_1 + K_{i2}u_2 + \cdots + (K_{ii} + \alpha)u_i + \cdots = F_i + \alpha \bar{u}_i$. If $\alpha$ is sufficiently large (e.g., $\alpha = 10^{10} \cdot \max \vert K_{ii} \vert$), the term $(K_{ii} + \alpha)u_i$ dominates the equation. The $i$-th equation effectively becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_i &\approx \frac{F_i + \alpha \bar{u}_i}{K_{ii} + \alpha} \longrightarrow \bar{u}_i \quad \text{as} \quad \alpha \to \infty
\end{aligned}$$

This proves that the penalty method enforces the BCs in the limit. Note that the reactions are **recovered** as the residual force:

$$\begin{aligned}
\{R\} &= \{F_p^{aug}\} - [K]\{U\} = (\{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}) - ([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U\} \\
&= \alpha[I_p](\{U_p\} - \{U\}) + \{F\}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example

**Problem:** A 2-DOF spring-mass system has $[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & -50 \\ -50 & 100 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$ and $\{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 10 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}$. The first DOF is fixed, i.e., $u_1 = 0$. Find $u_2$ and the reaction $R_1$ using both the Direct and Penalty methods.

### Solution via Direct (Partition) Method

**Step 1 — Identify prescribed and free DOFs.**
Prescribed: $u_1 = 0 = U_p$; Free: $u_2 = U_f$.

**Step 2 — Extract the reduced stiffness matrix $K_{ff}$.**
From the original $[K]$, the entry coupling free to free DOFs is the (2,2) entry:
$$[K_{ff}] = [100]$$

**Step 3 — Extract the coupling matrix $K_{fp}$.**
The entry coupling free DOF (2) to prescribed DOF (1) is $K_{21} = -50$:
$$[K_{fp}] = [-50]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the partition equation.**
$$[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} = \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
[100]\{u_2\} &= [10] - [-50][0] \\
100 \, u_2 &= 10 \\
u_2 &= 0.1 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Recover the reaction force $F_p$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\{F_p\} &= [K_{pf}]\{U_f\} + [K_{pp}]\{U_p\} \\
F_1 &= [-50][0.1] + [100][0] \\
F_1 &= -5 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

The negative sign indicates the reaction acts opposite to the assumed positive direction (i.e., the support pulls back on the mass).

### Solution via Penalty Method

Let $\alpha = 10^6 \text{ N/m}$.

**Step 1 — Construct the augmented system.**
$$([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} = \{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 100 + 10^6 & -50 \\ -50 & 100 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{Bmatrix} 0 + 10^6 \cdot 0 \\ 10 \end{Bmatrix} \\
\begin{bmatrix} 10^6 + 100 & -50 \\ -50 & 100 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 10 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Solve by inversion.**

The determinant is $(10^6 + 100)(100) - (-50)(-50) = 100^7 + 10^4 - 2500 \approx 10^8$.

Using Cramer's rule (or inverting):

$$\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \frac{(10^6 + 100)(10) - (-50)(0)}{10^8} = \frac{10^7 + 1000}{10^8} \approx 0.100000 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the Direct Method result, confirming convergence.

**Step 3 — Recover the reaction force.**
The reaction is the residual:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 = F_1 - (K_{11}u_1 + K_{12}u_2) &= 0 - \left[(100)(0) + (-50)(0.1)\right] = 5 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

(The sign is opposite because the reaction is the force the support exerts on the system.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** In the Direct Method, $\{F_p\}$ as computed is the **external load required to enforce** $\{U_p\}$. The actual reaction is the **negative** of this value when $\{U_p\} = \{0\}$. Always clarify whether the question asks for the *reaction force on the support* or *force exerted by the support*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of Boundary Condition Treatment

The following block-level flow diagram illustrates the complete decision pathway for treating BCs in a standard FEM workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Assembly"] --> B["Assemble Global K and F"]
    B --> C{"BC Type?"}
    C --> D["Essential BC Prescribed U"]
    C --> E["Natural BC Prescribed F"]
    E --> F["Add to Force Vector at Respective DOFs"]
    F --> G["Proceed to Solve System"]
    D --> H{"Choose Method?"}
    H --> I["Direct Partition Method"]
    H --> J["Penalty Method"]
    H --> K["Lagrange Multiplier"]
    I --> L["Partition K and F into Free and Prescribed"]
    L --> M["Extract Kff Sub-Matrix"]
    M --> N["Compute RHS as Ff minus Kfp times Up"]
    N --> O["Invert Kff to Find Uf"]
    O --> P["Back Substitute to Recover Reactions"]
    J --> Q["Construct Penalty Matrix alpha times Ip"]
    Q --> R["Augment System as K plus alpha Ip"]
    R --> S["Add alpha Up to Force Vector"]
    S --> T["Solve Full System in One Step"]
    T --> U["Extract Reactions as Residuals"]
    K --> V["Augment with Multiplier Unknowns Lambda"]
    V --> W["Solve Saddle Point System"]
    P --> X["End Post Processing"]
    U --> X
    W --> X
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Degrees of Freedom Classification

The following diagram maps the global DOF set into prescribed and free subsets, which is the foundational partition step.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph sAll["Global DOF Set of Size n"]
        sFree["Free DOFs Uf of Size f"]
        sPres["Prescribed DOFs Up of Size p"]
    end
    sAll --- sFree
    sAll --- sPres
    sFree --> nSolve["Unknown Solve for These"]
    sPres --> nKnown["Known Constants"]
    nSolve --> mat1["Kff governs equations"]
    nKnown --> mat2["Kfp cross couples them"]
    mat1 --> out1["Reduced system Kff Uf equals Ff minus Kfp Up"]
    mat2 --> out1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Penalty Method Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpK["Global K Matrix"] --> sum["Add Penalty alpha Ip"]
    inpPen["Penalty alpha scalar"] --> sum
    sum --> augK["Augmented Stiffness Matrix"]
    inpF["Force Vector F"] --> sumF["Add alpha Ip Up"]
    inpUp["Prescribed Up"] --> sumF
    sumF --> augF["Augmented Force Vector"]
    augK --> solve["Solve Linear System"]
    augF --> solve
    solve --> outU["Full Displacement Vector U"]
    outU --> res["Compute Residual Reaction Forces"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Essential** and **Natural** boundary conditions. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Essential Boundary Conditions** (also called Geometric or Dirichlet BCs) prescribe the value of the **primary variable** (e.g., displacement in structural problems, temperature in heat transfer) at the boundary. These conditions are imposed **before** or **after** assembling the global stiffness matrix.
- **Natural Boundary Conditions** (also called Force or Neumann BCs) prescribe the value of the **secondary variable** (e.g., traction/force, heat flux) and arise naturally from the weak/Galerkin formulation.
- **Example (Essential):** A fixed support in a cantilever beam imposing $u = 0$ at the wall.
- **Example (Natural):** A prescribed tip load $P$ at the free end of a cantilever beam.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the **penalty method** for applying boundary conditions in FEM? State one advantage and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**
- The penalty method applies essential boundary conditions by adding a **large numerical value $\alpha$** (penalty parameter) to the diagonal entries of the global stiffness matrix that correspond to prescribed DOFs. The augmented equation is $([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} = \{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}$, which approximately enforces $u_i = \bar{u}_i$ for large $\alpha$.
- **Advantage:** The structure of the stiffness matrix is preserved; no row/column elimination is required.
- **Disadvantage:** Choosing $\alpha$ is non-trivial — too small leads to BC violation, too large causes **numerical ill-conditioning** of the matrix.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module Internal Choice Option A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]** A bar is fixed at node 1 and subjected to an axial load at node 3. The global stiffness matrix and force vector for the 3-noded bar system (each element of length $L$, area $A$, Young's modulus $E$) after assembly are:

$$[K] = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \{F\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$$

The displacement at node 1 is prescribed: $u_1 = 0$. Using the **Direct (Partition) Method**:

**(a)** [7 Marks] Formulate the reduced stiffness equation and solve for the displacements $\{u_2, u_3\}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the reaction force at node 1.

#### Solution to Question 1(a)

**Step 1 — Identify prescribed and free DOFs.**
Prescribed: $u_1 = 0 = U_p$ (size $p = 1$). Free: $\{u_2, u_3\} = U_f$ (size $f = 2$).

**Step 2 — Partition the stiffness matrix.**

The sub-matrices are:
$$[K_{ff}] = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [K_{fp}] = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Partition the force vector.**
$\{F_f\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$, $\{U_p\} = [0]$.

**Step 4 — Write the reduced equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} &= \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\} \\
\frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_2 \\ u_3 \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix} - \frac{EA}{L}\begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}[0]
\end{aligned}$$

The RHS simplifies to $\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix}$ since $U_p = 0$.

**Step 5 — Solve by matrix inversion.**
The determinant of $K_{ff}$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\det(K_{ff}) &= \left(\frac{EA}{L}\right)^2 [(2)(1) - (-1)(-1)] = \left(\frac{EA}{L}\right)^2 (2 - 1) = \left(\frac{EA}{L}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

The inverse is:
$$[K_{ff}]^{-1} = \frac{L}{EA}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Compute displacements.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\{U_f\} &= [K_{ff}]^{-1}\{F_f\} = \frac{L}{EA}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ P \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{L}{EA}\begin{Bmatrix} P \\ 2P \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $u_2 = \frac{PL}{EA}$ and $u_3 = \frac{2PL}{EA}$.

#### Solution to Question 1(b)

**Step 1 — Use the back-substitution formula.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\{F_p\} &= [K_{pf}]\{U_f\} + [K_{pp}]\{U_p\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Extract the required sub-matrices.**
$[K_{pf}] = [K_{fp}]^T = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $[K_{pp}] = \frac{EA}{L}[2]$.

**Step 3 — Substitute known values.**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} PL/EA \\ 2PL/EA \end{Bmatrix} + \frac{EA}{L}[2][0] \\
&= \frac{EA}{L}\left(-\frac{PL}{EA}\right) + 0 \\
&= -P
\end{aligned}$$

The reaction force at node 1 is $R_1 = -F_1 = +P$, indicating the support exerts an upward (restoring) force of magnitude $P$ on the bar, balancing the applied load. This is consistent with global static equilibrium.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Sign Convention:** Many KTU students lose 2–3 marks by reporting $F_1 = -P$ as the "reaction" without clarifying that the physical reaction force on the structure is $+P$. Always state the direction explicitly: "$R_1 = +P$ acting in the positive $x$-direction to balance the applied load."

---

### Question 2 (Module Internal Choice Option B) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]** A 2-DOF system has the assembled equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 200 & -100 \\ -100 & 200 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 500 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ with } u_1 = 0$$

**(a)** [7 Marks] Solve the system using the **Penalty Method** with $\alpha = 10^{10}$. Verify the prescribed BC.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the reaction force and discuss why the penalty parameter must be "large but finite."

#### Solution to Question 2(a)

**Step 1 — Construct the penalty matrix $[P] = \alpha[I_p]$.**
Since $u_1$ is prescribed, the penalty is added at position (1,1):

$$[P] = 10^{10}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10^{10} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Form the augmented stiffness matrix.**

$$\begin{aligned}
[K_{aug}] &= [K] + [P] = \begin{bmatrix} 200 + 10^{10} & -100 \\ -100 & 200 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Form the augmented force vector.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\{F_{aug}\} &= \{F\} + [P]\{U_p\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 500 \end{Bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 10^{10} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ \cdot \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 500 \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

(Here, $\bar{u}_1 = 0$, so the second term vanishes.)

**Step 4 — Solve the augmented system.**

The determinant is:
$$\det(K_{aug}) = (10^{10} + 200)(200) - (-100)(-100) = 2 \times 10^{12} + 40000 - 10000 \approx 2 \times 10^{12}$$

Applying Cramer's rule:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -100 \\ 500 & 200 \end{bmatrix}}{\det(K_{aug})} = \frac{0 \cdot 200 - (-100)(500)}{2 \times 10^{12}} = \frac{50000}{2 \times 10^{12}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \\
u_2 &= \frac{\det\begin{bmatrix} 10^{10} + 200 & 0 \\ -100 & 500 \end{bmatrix}}{\det(K_{aug})} = \frac{(10^{10} + 200)(500)}{2 \times 10^{12}} \approx 2.5 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the BC.**
$u_1 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \approx 0$, confirming that the essential BC $u_1 = 0$ is enforced to machine precision.

#### Solution to Question 2(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the reaction force using the residual formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= F_1 - (K_{11}u_1 + K_{12}u_2) \\
&= 0 - [(200)(2.5 \times 10^{-8}) + (-100)(2.5)] \\
&= 0 - [5 \times 10^{-6} - 250] \\
&\approx 250 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Discuss the choice of $\alpha$.**
The penalty parameter must be **large but finite** for the following reasons:
- **If $\alpha$ is too small** (e.g., $\alpha < \max \vert K_{ii} \vert$): The diagonal augmentation becomes negligible, and the prescribed BC is violated. The error in $u_i$ scales as $O(1/\alpha)$.
- **If $\alpha$ is too large** (e.g., $\alpha \to \infty$ in floating-point arithmetic): The stiffness matrix becomes **severely ill-conditioned**. The **condition number** $\kappa([K_{aug}]) \approx \alpha / \min \vert K_{ii} \vert$ becomes enormous, causing **round-off errors** during LU decomposition and **loss of significant digits** in the solution.
- **Rule of Thumb in KTU Lab/Exams:** Choose $\alpha$ between $10^6 \cdot \max \vert K_{ii} \vert$ and $10^{10} \cdot \max \vert K_{ii} \vert$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Penalty Method:** KTU evaluators specifically check whether the student has written the **augmented equation** correctly with $\alpha$ appearing on **both** the LHS (in $[K]$) **and** the RHS (in $\{F\}$). Students frequently forget the RHS term, leading to a 4-mark penalty. Also, avoid stating $\alpha = \infty$; use a specific numerical value such as $10^{10}$ for clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boundary Conditions in FEA**

- **Two categories only:** Essential (geometric, primary, $u = \bar{u}$) and Natural (force, secondary, $q = \bar{q}$). They partition the boundary $\Gamma$ such that $\Gamma = \Gamma_u \cup \Gamma_q$ with $\Gamma_u \cap \Gamma_q = \emptyset$.

- **Three KTU-favoured methods for BC enforcement:** Direct (elimination), Penalty, and Partition. The Partition Method is a **variant** of the Direct Method that explicitly writes the reduced system $[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} = \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}$.

- **Master Equation to Memorize:** $\boxed{[K_{ff}]\{U_f\} = \{F_f\} - [K_{fp}]\{U_p\}}$ — this appears in nearly every KTU board problem.

- **Reactions are recovered via back-substitution:** $\{F_p\} = [K_{pf}]\{U_f\} + [K_{pp}]\{U_p\}$. Do not forget $[K_{pf}] = [K_{fp}]^T$.

- **Penalty Method Master Equation:** $\boxed{([K] + \alpha[I_p])\{U\} = \{F\} + \alpha[I_p]\{U_p\}}$. Choose $\alpha$ between $10^6$ and $10^{10}$ times the largest diagonal entry.

- **Sign Convention Awareness:** The reaction computed from $\{F_p\}$ in the Direct Method represents the *external* load required to enforce the displacement. The physical reaction force on the structure is its **negative** when the BC is $u = 0$.

- **Singularity Diagnosis:** A singular $[K]$ before BC application is the *hallmark* of an unconstrained system — the immediate remedy is to apply essential BCs.

- **Stiffness Matrix Symmetry:** Always preserved by the Direct Method; **preserved** by Penalty (if $[I_p]$ is symmetric diagonal); **NOT preserved** by Lagrange Multiplier (saddle-point structure).

- **Numerical Conditioning:** Penalty Method is **worse-conditioned** than Direct Method for large $\alpha$; Lagrange Multiplier gives **exact** enforcement but enlarges the system.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Treating natural BCs by modifying $[K]$ — this is wrong. Natural BCs only modify $\{F\}$, never $[K]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET632 - FINITE ELEMENT ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Effects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Effect (Temperature Effect) in FEA:** The response of a structural continuum to a change in temperature $\Delta T$, expressed mathematically through an *equivalent thermal load vector* $\{F_{\text{th}}\}$ and modified strain-displacement constitutive relation. When a body undergoes a temperature change, it experiences **thermal strain** $\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \Delta T$ (where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear thermal expansion). If this free expansion is partially or fully restrained by boundary conditions or by adjoining elements of differing stiffness/expansion, **thermal stresses** $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \alpha \Delta T$ develop.

In the Finite Element formulation, thermal effects are accommodated by **decomposing the total strain** into mechanical and thermal components:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} + \varepsilon_{\text{th}}
$$

The stress is then computed using only the mechanical portion of the strain:

$$
\sigma = E (\varepsilon_{\text{mech}}) = E(\varepsilon_{\text{total}} - \alpha \Delta T)
$$

This decomposition leads to an additional **equivalent nodal force vector** (thermal load vector) in the global equilibrium equation:

$$
[K]\{U\} = \{F_{\text{ext}}\} + \{F_{\text{th}}\}
$$

where $\{F_{\text{th}}\}$ is the *consistent thermal load vector* representing the internal forces generated by restrained thermal expansion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a metal ruler (say steel) lying freely on a table. When you heat it with a hair dryer, it visibly expands and slides along the table. **No internal stress develops** because there is no restraint — the ruler is free to expand. This is the **free thermal expansion** regime.

Now imagine the same ruler is **screwed tightly between two immovable walls**. As you heat it, the ruler tries to expand but cannot. The walls exert compressive forces on the ends of the ruler, and an internal **compressive stress** $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$ develops throughout its length. The walls "feel" reaction forces pushing outward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for FEA:** A *fully constrained* bar generates thermal stresses but **zero net displacement**. A *partially constrained* bar (e.g., one end fixed, other free) generates displacements but **zero thermal stress**. The FEA formulation must correctly handle both extremes through the equivalent thermal load vector.

**Real-World Engineering Examples:**
- 🚂 **Railway Tracks:** Rails expand in summer; expansion joints or pre-stress accommodates this. Without them, *thermal buckling* occurs.
- 🛢️ **Pipelines:** Hot oil in cold pipelines induces thermal stresses at anchor supports and bends.
- 🏗️ **Bridges:** Deck expansion joints accommodate $\sim 30$ mm of thermal movement on a 100 m span for a $50^{\circ}C$ rise.
- 🔩 **Composite Materials:** Mismatched $\alpha$ between fiber and matrix generates *thermal residual stresses* upon cooling from cure temperature.
- ✈️ **Aerospace Brackets:** Components bolted at room temperature but operating at $200^{\circ}C$ develop thermal clamping stresses.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Material Properties

| Material | Young's Modulus $E$ (GPa) | Coefficient of Thermal Expansion $\alpha$ ($\times 10^{-6}/^{\circ}C$) |
|----------|:---:|:---:|
| Steel | **200** | **12** |
| Aluminum | **70** | **23** |
| Concrete | **30** | **10** |
| Copper | **110** | **17** |
| Brass | **100** | **19** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - Length: mm, Force: N, Stress: MPa ($\text{N/mm}^2$)
> - Temperature change $\Delta T$ in °C (or K; the *difference* is numerically identical)
> - Coefficient $\alpha$ in $\text{per }^{\circ}C$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Strain Decomposition Principle

The cornerstone of thermal FEA is the **Principle of Strain Additivity** under the assumption of *small deformations* and *linear thermo-elasticity*. This principle states that the total strain in an element is the algebraic sum of strain due to mechanical loading and strain due to thermal expansion:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} + \varepsilon_{\text{th}}
$$

For a **1-D linear thermo-elastic bar element**:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \frac{du}{dx}, \quad \varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \Delta T(x), \quad \sigma = E \varepsilon_{\text{mech}}
$$

Therefore, the constitutive equation becomes:

$$
\sigma = E \left[ \frac{du}{dx} - \alpha \Delta T(x) \right]
$$

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic: Why an Equivalent Load Vector?

The **Principle of Minimum Potential Energy** for a body subjected to thermal effects yields:

$$
\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \sigma \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} \, dV - \int_L \{u\}^T \{f\} \, dx - \{U\}^T \{F_{\text{ext}}\}
$$

Substituting $\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = \varepsilon - \alpha \Delta T$ and minimizing:

$$
\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{U\}} = 0 \implies [K]\{U\} - \{F_{\text{th}}\} - \{F_{\text{ext}}\} = 0
$$

Rearranging gives the canonical FEA equation with thermal loading:

$$
[K]\{U\} = \{F_{\text{ext}}\} + \{F_{\text{th}}\}
$$

**Physical Interpretation of $\{F_{\text{th}}\}$:** These are the *nodal forces* that, if applied externally, would produce the same displacement field as the temperature change in a free body. Equivalently, they represent the forces required to *restrain* the free thermal expansion back to the original geometry.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Thermal Strain | $\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \Delta T$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Thermal Stress (fully constrained) | $\sigma_{\text{th}} = E \alpha \Delta T$ | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 3 | Thermal Force (free expansion) | $F_{\text{th}} = E A \alpha \Delta T$ | Newtons (N) |
| 4 | Element Stiffness Matrix (1D bar) | $[k] = \dfrac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | N/mm |
| 5 | Consistent Thermal Load Vector (uniform $\Delta T$) | $\{f_{\text{th}}\} = E A \alpha \Delta T \begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ +1 \end{Bmatrix}$ | N |
| 6 | Consistent Thermal Load Vector (linearly varying $\Delta T$ from $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$) | $\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \dfrac{EA \alpha L}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} -2\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 \\ +\Delta T_1 + 2\Delta T_2 \end{Bmatrix}$ | N |
| 7 | Strain-Displacement Matrix (1D) | $[B] = \dfrac{1}{L}\begin{bmatrix} -1 & +1 \end{bmatrix}$ | 1/mm |
| 8 | Shape Function Matrix (1D linear) | $[N] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - x/L & x/L \end{bmatrix}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Total Stiffness + Thermal Equation | $[K]\{U\} = \{F_{\text{ext}}\} + \{F_{\text{th}}\}$ | N |
| 10 | Thermal Force Vector via B-Matrix | $\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \int_V [B]^T E \alpha \Delta T \, dV$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** Positive $\Delta T$ (heating) generates a *compressive* thermal force at the supports. The thermal load vector pushes node 1 in the $-x$ direction and node 2 in the $+x$ direction, mimicking the natural tendency to expand.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal FEA is indispensable in:

1. **Pressure Vessel Design** — Operating temperature differs from installation temperature; supports experience sustained thermal load.
2. **Electronics Packaging** — Chip-on-board CTE mismatch generates fatigue stresses during thermal cycling.
3. **Nuclear Reactor Components** — Extreme thermal gradients combined with mechanical loads require coupled thermo-mechanical FEA.
4. **Turbine Blade Analysis** — Centrifugal + thermal + aerodynamic loads simultaneously analyzed.
5. **Additive Manufacturing Simulation** — Residual stresses from cooling phase predicted via thermal-structural sequential FEA.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Consistent Thermal Load Vector for a 1D Bar Element (Uniform $\Delta T$)

### Given:
A 1D bar element with:
- Length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$ (constant)
- Young's modulus $E$ (constant)
- Coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$ (constant)
- Uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$ across the element

### To Derive:
The element consistent thermal load vector $\{f_{\text{th}}\}$.

### Step 1: Define the Shape Functions

Using linear shape functions for a two-noded bar element:

$$
N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L}, \quad N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L}
$$

The displacement field is approximated as:

$$
u(x) = N_1(x) u_1 + N_2(x) u_2
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Strain-Displacement Matrix $[B]$

The total strain is the spatial derivative of the displacement:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{dN_1}{dx} u_1 + \frac{dN_2}{dx} u_2
$$

Computing the derivatives:

$$
\frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L}, \quad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L}
$$

Therefore, the $[B]$ matrix is:

$$
[B] = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Decompose Total Strain

Apply the strain decomposition:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} + \varepsilon_{\text{th}}
$$

Where:

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = [B]\{u\}, \quad \varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \Delta T
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Constitutive Relation

The mechanical stress is given by Hooke's law applied to the mechanical strain only:

$$
\sigma = E(\varepsilon_{\text{mech}}) = E\left([B]\{u\} - \alpha \Delta T\right)
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Principle of Virtual Work (or Minimum Potential Energy)

The internal virtual work for a thermally loaded element equals the external virtual work:

$$
\int_V [B]^T \sigma \, dV = \int_L [N]^T f_{\text{ext}} \, dx + \{f\}_{\text{point}}
$$

> **Note:** For the *consistent* thermal load derivation, we set $\{u\} = 0$ (no mechanical displacement) and equate the thermal stresses to an equivalent external force system.

Setting $\{u\} = 0$ in the stress expression:

$$
\sigma_{\text{residual}} = -E \alpha \Delta T \quad \text{(uniform across element)}
$$

### Step 6: Integrate to Obtain the Thermal Load Vector

Substituting into the equilibrium equation:

$$
\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \int_V [B]^T E \alpha \Delta T \, dV
$$

For *uniform* $\Delta T$, constant $E$, $A$, and $[B]$:

$$
\{f_{\text{th}}\} = [B]^T \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A \int_0^L dx
$$

The integral evaluates to $L$:

$$
\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A \cdot L
$$

### Step 7: Final Simplified Result

$$
\boxed{\{f_{\text{th}}\} = EA\alpha\Delta T \begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ +1 \end{Bmatrix}}
$$

> **Physical Sanity Check:** For $\Delta T > 0$, the force at node 1 is $-EA\alpha\Delta T$ (directed in $-x$), and at node 2 is $+EA\alpha\Delta T$ (directed in $+x$). This correctly represents the element's tendency to *expand* outward, pushing against any supports.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Element Stiffness Matrix (Standard Review)

The element stiffness matrix emerges from the strain energy of the mechanical strain component:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \sigma \, \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\{u\}^T \int_V [B]^T E [B] \, dV \, \{u\}
$$

Therefore:

$$
[k] = \int_V [B]^T E [B] \, dV
$$

Substituting $[B]$ and integrating over the element volume:

$$
[k] = A\int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} E \begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{1}{L} & \dfrac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} dx
$$

Evaluating the matrix product inside the integral:

$$
[k] = \frac{EA}{L^2} \int_0^L \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx
$$

The integrand is constant, so:

$$
\boxed{[k] = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Stepped Bar Fixed at Both Ends

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the canonical KTU Module 1 thermal problem.** Master this solution template.

### Problem Statement:
A stepped bar composed of two segments is rigidly fixed between two immovable walls. Both segments experience a uniform temperature rise of $\Delta T = 80^{\circ}C$.

**Element 1 (Steel):** $L_1 = 200$ mm, $A_1 = 300$ mm², $E_1 = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_1 = 12 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$

**Element 2 (Aluminum):** $L_2 = 300$ mm, $A_2 = 200$ mm², $E_2 = 70$ GPa, $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6} /^{\circ}C$

**Find:** Reactions at supports, internal thermal stresses.

### Solution:

#### Step 1: Element Stiffness Matrices

For Element 1 (Steel):

$$
k_1 = \frac{E_1 A_1}{L_1} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times 300}{200} = 3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}
$$

$$
[k_1] = 3.0 \times 10^5 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}
$$

For Element 2 (Aluminum):

$$
k_2 = \frac{E_2 A_2}{L_2} = \frac{70 \times 10^3 \times 200}{300} = 4.667 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}
$$

$$
[k_2] = 4.667 \times 10^4 \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/mm}
$$

#### Step 2: Thermal Load Vectors

For Element 1 (Steel):

$$
P_1 = E_1 A_1 \alpha_1 \Delta T = 200 \times 10^3 \times 300 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 80
$$

Computing the arithmetic:

$$
P_1 = 200000 \times 300 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 = 57600 \text{ N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\{f_{\text{th},1}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -57600 \\ +57600 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}
$$

For Element 2 (Aluminum):

$$
P_2 = E_2 A_2 \alpha_2 \Delta T = 70 \times 10^3 \times 200 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 80
$$

Computing the arithmetic:

$$
P_2 = 70000 \times 200 \times 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 = 25760 \text{ N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\{f_{\text{th},2}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -25760 \\ +25760 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 3: Assemble Global System

The global stiffness matrix with 3 nodes:

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_1 & -k_1 & 0 \\ -k_1 & k_1+k_2 & -k_2 \\ 0 & -k_2 & k_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
[K] = \begin{bmatrix} 3.000 \times 10^5 & -3.000 \times 10^5 & 0 \\ -3.000 \times 10^5 & 3.467 \times 10^5 & -4.667 \times 10^4 \\ 0 & -4.667 \times 10^4 & 4.667 \times 10^4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The assembled global thermal load vector:

$$
\{F_{\text{th}}\} = \begin{Bmatrix} -57600 \\ 57600 - 25760 \\ +25760 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} -57600 \\ 31840 \\ 25760 \end{Bmatrix} \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions

Both walls fixed: $U_1 = U_3 = 0$. Reducing to the single unknown $U_2$:

$$
(k_1 + k_2) U_2 = F_{\text{th},2,\text{internal}}
$$

$$
3.467 \times 10^5 \cdot U_2 = 31840
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{31840}{3.467 \times 10^5} = 0.0919 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute Reactions

The reaction at node 1 (left wall):

$$
R_1 = k_1 (U_1 - U_2) + (-P_1) = 3.0 \times 10^5 (0 - 0.0919) + (-57600)
$$

$$
R_1 = -27570 - 57600 = -85170 \text{ N}
$$

The reaction at node 3 (right wall):

$$
R_3 = k_2 (U_3 - U_2) + P_2 = 4.667 \times 10^4 (0 - 0.0919) + 25760
$$

$$
R_3 = -4289 + 25760 = +21471 \text{ N}
$$

**Equilibrium Check:** $\sum F = -85170 + 31840 + 21471 \cdot 2 = ?$ — Recheck:

$$
-85170 + 21471 = -63699; \quad \text{Internal} = 31840 \cdot (-1) = ? 
$$

Reassembling: Total external reactions must equal zero net (closed system):

$$
R_1 + R_3 = -85170 + 21471 = -63699 \text{ N}
$$

The internal node force at node 2 is:

$$
F_{\text{node2}} = -k_1 U_2 + (k_1 + k_2) U_2 - k_2 U_2 + 31840 = 31840 \text{ N (applied)}
$$

**Sanity check:** $R_1 + R_3 = -F_{\text{total applied}} \cdot (-1)$: The applied thermal forces sum to $-57600 + 25760 = -31840$ N, so reactions sum to $+31840$ N? — Wait, recompute reactions more carefully.

Better approach — apply equilibrium directly from element equations:

**Element 1 internal force** (steel):

$$
N_1 = k_1 (U_2 - U_1) - P_1 = 3.0 \times 10^5 (0.0919 - 0) - 57600 = 27570 - 57600 = -30030 \text{ N}
$$

**Element 2 internal force** (aluminum):

$$
N_2 = k_2 (U_3 - U_2) - P_2 = 4.667 \times 10^4 (0 - 0.0919) - 25760 = -4289 - 25760 = -30049 \text{ N}
$$

These should be equal (single internal axial force): $N_1 \approx N_2 \approx -30040$ N ✓ (small numerical discrepancy due to rounding).

**Reaction at node 1:** $R_1 = -N_1 = +30040$ N (compressive reaction on left wall)

**Reaction at node 3:** $R_3 = N_2 = -30040$ N (compressive reaction on right wall)

So both walls push inward with $\approx 30$ kN each. **Equilibrium holds:** $R_1 + R_3 = 0$ ✓

#### Step 6: Compute Thermal Stresses

Stress in steel:

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{N_1}{A_1} = \frac{-30040}{300} = -100.1 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

Stress in aluminum:

$$
\sigma_2 = \frac{N_2}{A_2} = \frac{-30040}{200} = -150.2 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via superposition:** For a fully constrained 1D composite bar:
> $$\sigma_i = E_i \alpha_i \Delta T \cdot \left(\frac{\text{axial force balance factor}}{1}\right)$$
> The actual stresses are *less* than the unrestrained $E\alpha\Delta T$ values because the constraint allows some elastic expansion.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Thermal Load Vector with Linearly Varying $\Delta T(x)$

When the temperature varies linearly along the element from $\Delta T_1$ at node 1 to $\Delta T_2$ at node 2:

$$
\Delta T(x) = N_1(x) \Delta T_1 + N_2(x) \Delta T_2
$$

The general thermal load integral becomes:

$$
\{f_{\text{th}}\} = [B]^T E A \int_0^L \Delta T(x) \, dx
$$

The integral of the linearly varying temperature:

$$
\int_0^L \Delta T(x) \, dx = \int_0^L \left[\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)\Delta T_1 + \frac{x}{L}\Delta T_2\right] dx = \frac{L}{2}(\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \frac{E A \alpha}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{2}(\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2) \begin{Bmatrix} -1 \\ +1 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Simplifying, the *consistent* thermal load vector with linearly varying temperature:

$$
\boxed{\{f_{\text{th}}\} = \frac{EA\alpha L}{6}\begin{Bmatrix} -(2\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2) \\ (\Delta T_1 + 2\Delta T_2) \end{Bmatrix}}
$$

> **Derivation Note:** This follows from integrating $[B]^T E \alpha \Delta T(x) A$ over the element length, with the $[B]$ matrix pulling out a $1/L$ factor and the linear $\Delta T(x)$ producing the $L/6$ weighting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: FEA Workflow with Thermal Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Geometry, Material E and alpha, Delta T] --> B[Discretize into Elements]
    B --> C[For Each Element]
    C --> D[Compute B Matrix from Shape Functions]
    D --> E[Compute Element Stiffness k = EA/L]
    E --> F[Compute Thermal Load f_th = EA alpha Delta T]
    F --> G[Assemble Global Stiffness K and Global Thermal Load F_th]
    G --> H[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    H --> I[Solve K times U = F_ext + F_th]
    I --> J[Extract Nodal Displacements U]
    J --> K[Compute Element Strains epsilon = B times U]
    K --> L[Compute Element Stresses sigma = E times epsilon - alpha times Delta T]
    L --> M[Post-Process: Reactions, Stress Contours]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Strain Decomposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Strain epsilon_total] --> B[Strain Decomposition]
    B --> C[Mechanical Strain epsilon_mech]
    B --> D[Thermal Strain alpha times Delta T]
    C --> E[Stress sigma = E times epsilon_mech]
    D --> F[Thermal Force Generation]
    F --> G[Equivalent Nodal Load F_th]
    G --> H[Global Equilibrium K U = F_ext + F_th]
    C --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Free vs. Constrained Thermal Expansion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Temperature Change Delta T applied] --> Q1{Is element constrained?}
    Q1 -->|NO: Free Expansion| P1[Thermal strain only: epsilon = alpha Delta T]
    P1 --> R1[No internal stress: sigma = 0]
    P1 --> S1[Free displacement: delta_L = alpha Delta T times L]
    Q1 -->|YES: Fully Fixed| P2[Strain suppressed: epsilon_total = 0]
    P2 --> R2[Pure thermal stress: sigma = E alpha Delta T]
    P2 --> S2[Zero displacement: delta_L = 0]
    Q1 -->|PARTIAL: One end fixed| P3[Mixed boundary value problem]
    P3 --> R3[Partial stress: sigma = lambda E alpha Delta T]
    P3 --> S3[Partial displacement: delta = 1-lambda times alpha Delta T times L]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Coupled Thermal-Structural Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Solve Heat Transfer Problem] --> B[Obtain Temperature Field Delta T of x y z]
    B --> C[Step 2: Map Thermal Result onto Structural Mesh]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Thermal Load Vector f_th at each element]
    D --> E[Step 4: Assemble K and F_th]
    E --> F[Step 5: Apply Mechanical BC and Mechanical Loads]
    F --> G[Step 6: Solve KU = F_ext + F_th]
    G --> H[Step 7: Recover Stresses sigma = E B U - alpha Delta T]
```

---

## 4.5 Physical Schematic: Thermal Loading of a Constrained 1D Bar

```
   Rigid Wall        Node 1       Interior       Node 2        Rigid Wall
       |               |             |              |               |
       |============= [u1=0] ==========[u2]==========[u3=0] ==========|
       |               |             |              |               |
       |          <-- F_th,1         |          F_th,2 -->          |
       |          = -EAαΔT          |          = +EAαΔT              |
       |               |             |              |               |
       |               v             |              v               |
       |           Reaction R1       |         Reaction R2            |
       |               ^             |              ^               |
       |               |             |              |               |
       |          Compressive stress develops: σ_th = EαΔT          |
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows from the walls indicate the reactions pushing back against thermal expansion. The thermal load vector represents these reactions distributed to the element nodes.

---

## 4.6 Material Property Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Material Database] --> B[Steel: E=200 GPa, alpha=12e-6]
    A --> C[Aluminum: E=70 GPa, alpha=23e-6]
    A --> D[Copper: E=110 GPa, alpha=17e-6]
    A --> E[Concrete: E=30 GPa, alpha=10e-6]
    B --> F[Computed Properties]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[E alpha = Thermal Stress Coefficient]
    F --> H[E A alpha L = Thermal Force Scale]
```
